claims.claim: Among all ISC scenarios, the Al-anode ISC generates the largest power and leads to the highest cell temperatures due to the elimination of the cathode (the electrical bottleneck) from the current pathway.
claims.quote: Among all the ISC scenarios, the Al-anode ISC scenario generates the largest power and leads to the highest cell temperatures. This is due to the elimination of the electrical bottleneck, cathode, from the current pathway.

claims.claim: In the Al-anode ISC scenario, the maximum temperature attained (~1180 °C) is more than one order of magnitude higher than in cathode–anode and Cu-cathode ISC scenarios (~70 °C), with the latter two differing by less than 2 °C in peak temperature.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature attained in the Al-anode ISC (∼1180 ○C) is more than one order of magnitude higher than the other two scenarios (∼70 ○C). Cathode–anode and Cu-cathode ISC scenarios behave like each other with less than 2 ○C difference between their peak temperatures.

claims.claim: The Al-anode ISC has an electrical resistance of 2.4 Ω compared to 87 Ω in Cu-cathode ISC and 95 Ω in cathode–anode ISC, and this lower resistance is the primary mechanism for triggering thermal runaway via higher electrochemical heat generation rate.
claims.quote: the Al-anode ISC has an electrical resistance of 2.4 Ω as compared to the 87 Ω in the Cu-cathode ISC and 95 Ω in the cathode–anode ISC. Hence, the lower electrical resistance of the Al-anode pathway leads to a much higher electrochemical heat generation rate, which is the primary mechanism for triggering thermal runaway.

claims.claim: For cathode–anode ISC with a fixed cell outer radius of 0.72 mm, there is a critical nail radius between 5 and 10 μm beyond which thermal runaway occurs; a 5 μm nail does not trigger TR while a 10 μm nail does.
claims.quote: there is a critical nail radius, only beyond which the cell undergoes TR... A 10 μm nail successfully triggers the catastrophic event, and hence, it can be concluded that for this cell configuration and geometry, the critical electrochemical heat generation rate lies between 0.4 and 0.9 mW.

claims.claim: Smaller cells are more susceptible to thermal runaway than larger cells with the same ISC type and nail radius because the initial electrochemical heat generation rate is independent of cell capacity while smaller cells have smaller heat dissipation surface areas.
claims.quote: smaller cells are more vulnerable to TR as compared to larger cells in the same configuration... Even though the total electrochemical heat generated in a larger cell is higher, the initial rate of heat generation is still the same if the nail radius and the ISC type are kept fixed... The smaller capacity cells also have smaller surface areas for heat dissipation.

claims.claim: The ISC current is not homogeneously distributed across the entire cell; only a small radial region in both active material layers contributes to vertical current transport, and the effective conduction radius is slightly larger than the nail radius and increases monotonically with nail radius.
claims.quote: Most of the current traveling vertically in the active layers is constrained within a small region whose radius is slightly larger than the radius of the penetrating filament... The effective radius (reff) is a function of the nail radius and increases monotonically with the same.

claims.claim: During Cu–Al ISC, hot spots are virtually non-existent due to the high thermal conductivity of Al and Cu and the minimal power contribution from current collectors, so despite high ISC current, Cu–Al ISC leads to lower cell temperatures than other ISC scenarios.
claims.quote: During Cu–Al ISC, these hot spots are virtually non-existent, due to the high thermal conductivity of Al and Cu, and the minimal share of power contribution from the current collectors. Hence, despite the high ISC current, Cu–Al ISCs lead lower cell temperatures than their counterparts.

claims.claim: The empirical ISC model developed in this study achieves a Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of 45.7% compared to 105.9% for the prior state-of-the-art ISC model when validated against experimental TR propagation data, representing a ~60% reduction in MAPE.
claims.quote: our ISC model shows a Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of 45.7% as compared to the past ISC model, which has an MAPE of 105.9% when validated against their experiments. Hence, our empirical ISC model reduces the MAPE by ∼60%.

claims.claim: The empirical ISC model is calibrated for a specific battery and cannot be applied directly to a different battery; any change in cell capacity, cell geometry, or cell chemistry requires recalibration.
claims.quote: It is an empirical model, which captures the general trend of heat generation during an ISC even in LIBs. Hence, a model calibrated for a particular battery cannot be applied directly to a different one. Any change in the following parameters demands a recalibration of the model: cell capacity, cell geometry (pouch, cylindrical, prismatic, etc.,), cell chemistry.

claims.claim: A simplified 1D series electrical resistance network fails to predict ISC resistance behavior: doubling the nail radius from 5 to 10 μm yields a theoretical resistance change of 0.3% but simulations show a 56% drop, and doubling cell radius from 0.72 to 1.44 mm yields a theoretical change of 74% but simulations show only 1% change.
claims.quote: When we double the nail radius from 5 to 10 μm, the theoretical change in overall network resistance should be 0.3%, whereas simulations indicate a resistance drop of 56%. Similarly, when the cell radius is doubled from 0.72 to 1.44 mm, the theoretical change in overall network resistance should be 74%, but the simulations indicate a change of 1%.

claims.claim: After thermal runaway, the vertical thermal conductivity of the jellyroll decreased by 38.9% (from 0.548 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹ to 0.335 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹) and the parallel thermal conductivity decreased by 48.6% (from 15.9 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹ to 8.137 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹) for a 37 Ah NCM/Graphite prismatic cell.
claims.quote: After TR, the vertical and parallel thermal conductivity of the jellyroll decreased 38.9 % and 48.6 %, respectively.

claims.claim: After thermal runaway, the mass loss of the 37 Ah NCM/Graphite prismatic cell was approximately 35% (from 830 g to 602 g), corresponding to a decrease in density from 2680 kg·m⁻³ to 1760 kg·m⁻³.
claims.quote: The mass loss, which corresponding to the decrease in density, is approximately 35 %, whereas the value of specific heat capacity is only slightly diminished, by a mere 2.5 %.

claims.claim: After thermal runaway, the specific heat capacity of the battery core decreased by only 2.5%, from 1090.2 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ to 1065.7 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹, for a 37 Ah NCM/Graphite prismatic cell.
claims.quote: the specific heat capacity of the battery decreases from 1090.2 J⋅kg−1⋅K−1 to 1065.7 J⋅kg−1⋅K−1 after TR

claims.claim: A thermal runaway propagation model that ignores post-TR parameter changes (Model 2) predicts propagation times approximately 62% shorter than experimentally observed, whereas a model accounting for parameter changes (Model 1) predicts propagation times within ~9% of experiment.
claims.quote: the results of Model 2 deviates from the experiment with faster failure propagation... t2–3: Experiment 111 s, Model 1 121 s (+9.1 %), Model 2 42 s (−62.6 %)

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, battery venting causes the liquid electrolyte to leave via vaporization and decomposition, transforming the core from a porous structure into pure solid consisting of copper foil and mixed residual debris with a loose quasi-laminated anisotropic structure.
claims.quote: During the process of TR, liquid electrolyte leaves the battery due to vaporization and decomposition, causing the core to transform from porous into pure solid. The core wreckage consists of copper foil and mixed residual debris, with a loose quasi-laminated structure that has anisotropic thermal conductivity.

claims.claim: The maximum internal temperature of a large-format prismatic lithium-ion cell can reach 896.7°C after thermal runaway, with self-heating onset at 104.5°C and thermal runaway triggering temperature at 250.6°C as measured by ARC.
claims.quote: The self-heating temperature Tonset is 104.5 °C, and the TR triggering temperature TTR is 250.6 °C. After TR triggering, the battery reacts violently until it reaches the highest temperature Tmax = 896.7 °C.

claims.claim: The reduction of thermal conductivity after thermal runaway slows heat transfer from the failed cell to neighboring cells, while the decrease in density and heat capacity reduces the thermal energy stored in the post-TR cell, both affecting thermal runaway propagation behavior.
claims.quote: the decrease of battery density and heat capacity in Model 1 leads to the reduction of thermal energy stored in the battery after TR... the reduction of thermal conductivity in Model 1 slows down the heat transfer between neighboring cells.

claims.claim: The temperature difference between the interior and exterior of a lithium-ion battery can reach 13.16°C and 17.17°C respectively at room temperature during normal charging.
claims.quote: The temperature difference between the interior and exterior can reach 13.16 C respectively at room temperature. ... C and 17.17

claims.claim: Under different charging and discharging rates and ambient temperatures, a strong linear correlation is observed between the internal and external temperatures of the battery, and the slope remains consistent across different charge and discharge rates.
claims.quote: under different charging and discharging rates and ambient temperatures, a strong linear correlation is observed between the internal and external temperatures of the battery. The slope remains consistent across different charge and discharge rates.

claims.claim: The temperature factor k, defined as (Tin - Tamb)/(Tout - Tamb), varies from 1.46 to 1.88 depending on ambient temperature, and is independent of charge/discharge rate.
claims.quote: With respect to different ambient temperatures, the temperature factor exhibits variability ranging from 1.46 to 1.88.

claims.claim: When the internal battery temperature reaches 80°C (ambient temperature = 25°C), the corresponding external battery temperature is 62°C, corresponding to the electrolyte evaporation stage and the first-level thermal runaway warning threshold.
claims.quote: When the internal battery temperature is 80 C (ambient temperature = 25 C), the corresponding external battery temperature is 62 C.

claims.claim: When the internal battery temperature reaches 100°C (ambient temperature = 25°C), the corresponding external battery temperature is 76°C, corresponding to SEI decomposition onset and the second-level thermal runaway warning threshold.
claims.quote: When the internal temperature is 100 C, the corresponding external temperature of the battery is 76 C (ambient temperature = 25 C).

claims.claim: Gas monitoring based on gas discharged during thermal runaway is not an early warning of thermal runaway, as it occurs after the event has already initiated.
claims.quote: Gas monitoring was based on the gas discharged during thermal runaway, which is not an early warning of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Warning based on the critical value of thermal runaway belongs to delayed warning because once battery temperature reaches the critical point of thermal runaway, it becomes inevitable for the battery to undergo thermal runaway.
claims.quote: In reality, once the battery temperature reaches the critical point of thermal runaway, it becomes inevitable for the battery to undergo thermal runaway. The warning based on the critical value of thermal runaway belongs to the delayed warning.

claims.claim: After embedding thermocouples via drilling, charge capacity, discharge capacity, charge internal resistance, and discharge internal resistance decreased by 0.4%, 0.8%, 3.0%, and 3.2% respectively, possibly due to electrolyte volatilization.
claims.quote: the charge and discharge capacity and internal resistance decreased by 0.4 %, 0.8 %, 3.0 %, and 3.2 % respectively after the battery was drilled into the thermocouple, which may be caused by the volatilization of the electrolyte.

claims.claim: During discharge at rates above 2C, heat generation is much larger than during charging at the same rate, with the heat generation difference reaching 1068 Wh at 7C.
claims.quote: When charging and discharging above 2C rate, the heat generation by discharging was much larger than that by charging. When charging and discharging at 7C, the heat generation difference reached 1068 Wh.

claims.claim: The temperature factor k follows two distinct empirical relationships with ambient temperature: k = 1.86 + 0.01·Tamb for Tamb in (-20°C, 0°C), and k = 1.52 + 0.36·e^(-0.24·Tamb) for Tamb in (0°C, 60°C).
The critical ambient temperature (Ta,cr) for self-heating ignition of a single 18650 battery with LCO, NCM, and LFP as positive electrode materials are 148°C, 168°C, and 201°C, respectively.
claims.quote: the Ta,cr of LCO, NCM, and LFP battery cells are respectively 148 ◦C, 168 ◦C and 201 ◦C

claims.claim: The critical ambient temperature (Ta,cr) of a battery pack decreases as the number of cells increases, with the decreasing trend being fast at first and then slowing down.
claims.quote: as the number of batteries increases, the Ta,cr of battery pack decreases. The decreasing trend is fast at first and then slows down.

claims.claim: For a 64-cell LCO battery pack, the Ta,cr increases as the arrangement becomes more elongated and sparse: 8×8 staggered (118°C), 8×8 (119°C), 4×16 (120°C), and 2×32 (123°C).
claims.quote: the Ta,cr of battery packs in array 1, array 2, array 3 and array 4 are respectively 118 ◦C, 119 ◦C, 120 ◦C and 123 ◦C. This means that the more elongated and sparse the array, the higher the Ta,cr of battery pack will be.

claims.claim: The aerogel layer does not increase the Ta,cr of the battery pack compared to natural convection (both 119°C), but it delays the onset time of thermal runaway from 15.322 h to 16.669 h.
claims.quote: the Ta,cr of battery packs in natural state, equipped with aerogel layer, aluminum plate and graphite composite plate are 119 ◦C, 119 ◦C, 121 ◦C and 121 ◦C, respectively... the aerogel layer cannot enhance the Ta,cr of the battery pack, it can delay the time of thermal runaway of the module.

claims.claim: The graphite composite plate, which has a sandwich structure with high thermal conductivity outer layers (plane/axial: 800/25 W/m/K) and a low thermal conductivity middle layer (0.02 W/m/K), increases the battery pack Ta,cr to 121°C and delays thermal runaway onset to 18.581 h, and is identified as more suitable than Al plate and aerogel layer for battery pack storage and transportation.
claims.quote: the graphite composite plate has a sandwich structure, which does not use the outer battery to balance the temperature of the inner battery like the Al plate... the graphite composite plate is also a very light material, so it is the best choice in the storage and transportation of lithium battery packs among these three plates.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in a battery pack propagates from the center to the periphery regardless of the arrangement mode, meaning arrangement mode affects Ta,cr but not the thermal runaway propagation sequence within the module.
claims.quote: thermal runaway occurs first in the center position and later in the edge position. This shows that the arrangement mode only affects the Ta,cr of battery packs, and does not affect the sequence of thermal runaway of the battery inside the module.

claims.claim: When the ambient temperature is less than Ta,cr, the battery or battery pack cannot go to thermal runaway by self-heating; when the ambient temperature equals or exceeds Ta,cr, self-heating ignition can occur.
claims.quote: When the ambient temperature is equal to or larger than Ta,cr, the battery or battery pack can go to thermal runaway by self-heating. When the ambient temperature is less than Ta,cr, the battery or battery pack cannot go to thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The Al plate (thermal conductivity 230 W/m/K) raises the battery pack Ta,cr to 121°C and reduces the maximum temperature difference within the battery pack to only 20°C, indicating improved temperature uniformity.
claims.quote: When the battery pack is equipped with Al plate, the maximum temperature difference of the battery pack is only 20 ◦C. The results show that the Al plate can well uniform the temperature distribution of the battery pack.

claims.claim: Battery self-heating begins at 70–90 °C due to decomposition of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film.
claims.quote: When the temperature rises up to 70–90 °C, the battery starts self-heating due to the decomposition of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film

claims.claim: Cathode active material decomposition and oxygen release begin at approximately 250 °C, after which reactions induced by released oxygen contribute to total battery heat generation.
claims.quote: The reactions in sample Cat+An began to contribute to the total battery heat generation after the temperature reached 250 °C

claims.claim: The heat generation of a Li(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)O2/graphite cell before 250 °C is dominated by reactions on the anode/electrolyte interface, specifically SEI film decomposition and reaction between anode active material and electrolyte.
claims.quote: the battery heat generation before 250 °C was dominated by the reactions on the anode/electrolyte interface, such as the decomposition of SEI film, and the reaction between anode active material and electrolyte

claims.claim: Combustion of flammable gases occurs outside the battery and has little influence on battery temperature response because combustion energy heats the surroundings rather than the cell.
claims.quote: the combustion always occurs outside the batteries and has little inﬂuence on battery temperature response, as the combustion energy is not fully utilized to heat the cell, but heat the surroundings

claims.claim: Dahn's TR model could predict the onset of battery thermal runaway but failed to predict the maximum temperature because not all exothermic reactions were included.
claims.quote: Dahn's model could predict the onset of battery TR well when comparing to the oven tests, but failed in the prediction of the maximum temperature, since not all the exothermic reactions were included in the model

claims.claim: A decline in battery temperature rate observed experimentally at around 115 °C results from vaporization of the electrolyte and subsequent rupture of the pouch bag, an effect not captured by the TR model.
claims.quote: a decline of the battery temperature rate was observed in the experiment results at around 115 °C, resulting from the vaporization of the electrolyte and subsequent rupture of pouch bag, while the predicted temperature rate proﬁle shows no decrease as the eﬀects of electrolyte vaporization are not taken into consideration yet

claims.claim: The sample Anode+Electrolyte (An+Ele) total heat generation was 940.4 J g⁻¹, and the sample Cathode+Anode (Cat+An) total heat generation was 1446.7 J g⁻¹, making these the two dominant heat source pairs during battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The exothermic reactions between anode and electrolyte and those between cathode and anode turned out to be the dominated heat sources during battery TR process, as sample An+Ele and sample Cat+An exhibited significant heat generation (926.0 J g⁻¹ and 1446.8 J g⁻¹, respectively)

claims.claim: The heat generation of sample Cathode+Electrolyte (Cat+Ele, 428.1 J g⁻¹) was dominated by self-decomposition of cathode active material, with the reaction between cathode active material and electrolyte producing limited heat, mainly due to solvent vaporization before oxygen release at around 250 °C.
claims.quote: the heat generation of sample Cat+Ele was dominated by the self-decomposition of cathode active material, while the reaction between cathode active material and electrolyte produced limited heat, mainly due to the vaporization of the solvents... before the release of oxygen from the cathode active material at around 250 °C

claims.claim: The sharp experimental rise of battery temperature rate to more than 2000 °C min⁻¹ after thermal runaway onset is attributed to possible massive internal short circuit and violent reactions of all cell components, which are not included in the TR model.
claims.quote: The sharp rise of experimental battery temperature rate is due to possible massive internal short circuit and the violent reactions of all the cell components in a short time... which are diﬃcult to simulate based on the investigations of cell components, and are thus not included in the TR model yet

claims.claim: For the 130 °C oven test, the battery did not reach full thermal runaway and the maximum temperature reached approximately 180 °C, whereas for the 150 °C oven test the battery ran into thermal runaway with a maximum temperature of 857.74 °C.
claims.quote: The predicted Tmax during the 130 °C oven test reaches 185.84 °C, consistent with the experimental result (179.43 °C). For the 150 °C oven test, the battery ran into TR after heated to 150 °C and resting for 30 min... The maximum temperature Tmax reached 857.74 °C

claims.claim: In the EV-ARC test, the onset of self-heating temperature T1 is defined as the internal temperature when dT/dt reaches 0.02 °C/min, and the triggering temperature T2 is defined as the internal temperature when dT/dt reaches 1 °C/s.
claims.quote: When dT/dt arrives at the threshold value, i.e., 0.02 °C/min, the temperature obtained from the implanted thermocouple is defined as the onset of self-heating temperature T1... The temperature of the implanted thermocouple at this moment is defined as the triggering temperature T2.

claims.claim: For a commercial prismatic NMC/graphite cell (60 Ah), the characteristic temperatures measured by the internal thermocouple during EV-ARC test are T1 = 72.4 °C, T2 = 222.7 °C, and T3 = 994.8 °C.
claims.quote: the characteristic temperatures of T1, T2 and T3 are determined from an internal thermocouple and they are 72.4 °C, 222.7 °C and 994.8 °C, respectively.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature variation rate measured by the internal thermocouple (531 °C/s) is greater than that measured by the surface thermocouple (49.2 °C/s) by approximately one order of magnitude.
claims.quote: the temperature variation rate related to the central thermocouple accelerates once temperature exceeds T1, and it can go up to the maximum of 531 °C/s shortly after T2... the temperature variation rate related to the surface thermocouple is much less by one order of magnitude, and the maximum is only 49.2 °C/s.

claims.claim: The battery cell voltage drops sharply from 3.22 V to 0.25 V within 1 s, and this voltage dive occurs 6.8 s before the onset of thermal runaway, providing an early warning signal.
claims.quote: the voltage of the battery cell goes down mildly over a long time before the temperature reaches T2, and it jumps down sharply from 3.22 V to 0.25 V within 1 s. Furthermore, it is important to observe that the dive of voltage is 6.8 s before the onset of TR.

claims.claim: In the battery pack energy flow analysis, approximately 41% of total cell energy heats the cell itself, ~47% is ejected by venting, ~7.8% transfers forward to the next battery cell, and ~1.8% transfers to the coolant by convection.
claims.quote: about 41 % of the total energy is used to heat the battery cell itself, and nearly 47 % of the total energy is ejected out by venting, and about 7.8 % transfers forward to the next battery cell but a small proportion transfers backward to the previous one, and about 1.8 % transfers to coolant by convection.

claims.claim: The TR propagation time interval associated with silicon rubber pads (~120 s) is much greater than the time interval between adjacent battery cells (~60 s) within the same battery set.
claims.quote: the TR propagation time interval related to a silicon rubber pad (around 120 s) is much greater than that between adjacent battery cells (around 60 s).

claims.claim: Under uphill climbing conditions, the initial battery cell temperature before TR onset (47.4 °C) is higher than under fast charging (47.2 °C) and rapid heating with charging (28.0 °C), and the uphill climbing condition produces the shortest TR propagation time intervals among the four conditions studied.
claims.quote: the final temperatures of battery cells after these three severe working conditions are different, that is, 28.0 °C, 47.2 °C and 47.4 °C after the condition of rapid heating, fast charging and uphill climbing, respectively... the uphill climbing is the worst among the four.

claims.claim: The effect of venting gases and high-temperature particles on TR behavior inside the battery housing is not covered by the present model, representing a limitation of the study.
claims.quote: As the venting gases and particles from battery cells during TR are with high temperature, the surrounding temperature inside the battery housing would increase in a short time, and this would result in a significant impact on TR behaviors of batteries. Investigation into this phenomenon is not covered by the present work.

claims.claim: The SEI layer becomes unstable and exothermically decomposes after 333.15–393.15 K (60–120 °C).
claims.quote: This layer becomes unstable and exothermically decomposes after 333.15–393.15 K (60-120 ◦C)

claims.claim: The negative-electrolyte decomposition reaction occurs between 393.15–523.15 K (120–250 °C).
claims.quote: for the negative-electrolyte reaction between 393.15–523.15 K (120–250 ◦C)

claims.claim: The positive-electrolyte decomposition reaction occurs around 403.15–443.15 K (130–170 °C).
claims.quote: for the positive-electrolyte reaction around 403.15 K–443.15 K (130–170 ◦C)

claims.claim: Electrolyte decomposition usually occurs at temperatures near 473.15 K (200 °C).
claims.quote: the electrolyte usually decomposes at temperatures near 473.15 K (200 ◦C)

claims.claim: A lower SoC leads to less reactive LIBs and longer thermal runaway incubation periods, whereas a higher ambient temperature increases the likelihood of TR.
claims.quote: A lower SoC leads to less reactive LIBs and longer TR incubation periods, whereas a higher ambient temperature increases the likelihood of TR.

claims.claim: Polarization heat is dominant at lower discharge rates, whereas ohmic heat is dominant at higher discharge rates in an 18650 LIB.
claims.quote: The results suggest that polarization heat is dominant at lower discharge rates, whereas ohmic heat is dominant at higher discharge rates.

claims.claim: Entropic heat represents approximately 50% of total heat generated at a 2/3C rate and 39% at a 1C rate.
claims.quote: entropic heat cannot be ignored, as it represents approximately 50% of total heat generated for a 2/3C rate and 39% for a 1C rate.

claims.claim: In the LG HG2 simulation at 6C discharge-charge, the heat generation from Qtot,H (Hatchard) becomes the predominant heat source once surface temperature exceeds 426.62 K (153.47 °C), generating 59.23 W versus 2.4 W from Newman's model.
claims.quote: the heat generation from Qtot,H (Hatchard) becomes the predominant heat source once the surface temperature of the battery exceeds 426.62 K (153.47 ◦C) (from t = 1350 s to t = 1500 s, Qtot,N (Newman): 2.4 W, Qtot,H (Hatchard): 59.23 W)

claims.claim: For the LG HG2 battery under oven test conditions, heat generation due to Qtot,H (Hatchard) process rapidly increases from 71.4 W at the TR onset temperature to 20.6 kW at the end of the TR process in 16.9 s.
claims.quote: heat generation due to Qtot,H (Hatchard) process rapidly increases from 71.4 W at the TR onset temperature to 20.6 kW at the end of the TR process in 16.9 s.

claims.claim: The maximum battery surface temperature increased by approximately 90 K per 25% increase in SoC during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: In [59] it is found that the maximum battery surface temperature increased by approximately 90 K per 25% increase in SoC.

claims.claim: Melting of the separator layer at high temperatures yields direct contact between the negative and positive electrode layers, producing internal short circuits.
claims.quote: Melting of the SP layer at high temperatures yields direct contact between the NE and PE layers, producing ISC.

claims.claim: For the LG HG2 battery at 6C cycling, the SEI decomposition onset temperature (T1) was observed at 347.69 K (74.54 °C) after 145 s from simulation start.
claims.quote: the SEI decomposition (T1) is observed to occur at an onset temperature of 347.69 K (74.54 ◦C) after 145 s from the start of the simulation.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway onset temperature T2 for the LG HG2 battery is defined by a temperature change rate of 48 K/min and occurred at 445.43 K (172.28 °C).
claims.quote: T2 occurs at 445.43 K (172.28 ◦C) 10 s after the electrolyte starts to decompose (temperature change of 48 K/min for the LG HG2)

claims.claim: For the pouch (LiFePO4) battery at 6C cycling, Qtot,H (Hatchard) generates 3.4 times more heat than Qtot,N (Newman) after the electrolyte onset temperature, leading to a fast temperature rise.
claims.quote: after the onset temperature of the electrolyte, 𝑄tot,H (Hatchard) generates 3.4 times more heat than 𝑄tot,N (Newman) process, leading to a fast temperature rise.

claims.claim: The model's prediction of thermal runaway process is limited in that changes in thermophysical properties of the LIB after T3 (maximum temperature) are not captured.
claims.quote: Due to limitations in the model, changes in the LIB's thermophysical properties after T3 are not captured.

claims.claim: A prior machine/deep learning model for TR prediction under operating conditions is limited to the heat generation contribution from SEI decomposition, neglecting other significant heat-generation reactions.
claims.quote: the model is limited to the contribution to heat generation due to the decomposition of the SEI, neglecting other significant heat–generation reactions.

claims.claim: Temperature prediction accuracy increases with increasing layers in modeling as compared to traditional single-layer models.
claims.quote: Alipour et al. [9] showed that temperature prediction accuracy increases with increasing layers in modeling as compared to traditional single–layer models.

claims.claim: The ISC heat generation depends on battery SoC, cell voltage, and battery capacity with a non-Arrhenius rate.
claims.quote: This process quickly releases energy internally with a non–Arrhenius rate, which depends on the battery's SoC, voltage (𝑉cell), and battery capacity (𝐶cell).

claims.claim: For the LG HG2 battery in the oven test, the critical temperature at which TR starts is 454.65 K (181.5 °C) and the maximum battery surface temperature is 996.15 K (723 °C).
claims.quote: The critical temperature at which TR starts and the maximum battery surface temperature reported in [24] are 454.65 K (181.5 ◦C) and 996.15 K (723 ◦C), respectively.

claims.claim: Using an extra coarse mesh, the solution time for a 15-minute battery cycle is reduced to just 8 minutes, with surface temperature prediction accuracy deteriorating by only a few percent compared to finer meshes.
claims.quote: Although the surface temperature prediction accuracy deteriorates by a few percent, the solution time of a 15–minute cycle is reduced to just 8 min with this mesh.

claims.claim: During nail penetration at 30% SOC and 2 mm/s, voltage exhibits a stair-step decrease with increasing penetration depth, with the maximum voltage drop of 1.53 V occurring in the first unit cell.
claims.quote: With deeper penetration, the voltage decreases in a step-wise manner, implying a strong correlation between voltage drop and the number of ISC unit cells. Comparing the voltage changes in 5 unit cells, the maximum voltage drop (1.53 V) occurs in the first unit cell.

claims.claim: When the nail punctures the first unit cell separator, the short-circuit current surges rapidly to approximately 47 A as all non-short-circuited unit cells discharge through the first short-circuit point.
claims.quote: As the nail pierces the first unit cell, it forms a loop with short-circuit current Is-1. At this point, all non-short-circuited unit cells discharge through the short-circuit point of the first unit cell, leading to a rapid surge in short-circuit current to about 47 A.

claims.claim: The rupture of the separator triggers formation of a high-temperature hotspot at the current collector of the affected unit cell, causing a temperature peak.
claims.quote: The rupture of the separator leads to the formation of a hotspot with high-temperature at the current collector of the affected unit cell. As the nail progresses through subsequent unit cell's separator, new hotspots could be generated.

claims.claim: At SOC = 90%, the maximum voltage drop during the first ISC event can reach approximately 2 V, compared to approximately 1.5 V at SOC = 30%, because higher SOC leads to larger short-circuit current.
claims.quote: For the 90 % SOC case, the voltage drop during first ISC can reach approximately 2 V, while for 30 % SOC, it is around 1.5 V. Since the cell internal resistance remains consistent for different SOC, a higher SOC leads to a larger short-circuit current, resulting in greater voltage drop.

claims.claim: At SOC = 90%, the cell experiences a maximum temperature rise of 820°C during nail penetration ISC, compared to lower rises at lower SOC values.
claims.quote: The cell experiences a maximum temperature rise of 820 ◦C as the SOC increases from 30 % to 90 %.

claims.claim: At a slow nail puncture speed of 0.5 mm/s, the Li+ transport reaches the 'ion transport limit' at a relatively short puncture distance (d = 0.224 mm), causing a rapid temperature rise followed by descent after peak temperature.
claims.quote: When v = 0.5 mm/s, the Li+ transport reaches the 'ion transport limit' at a relatively short puncture distance (d = 0.224 mm). This leads to a rapid temperature rise mode in the cell, followed by a descent after reaching the peak temperature.

claims.claim: For nail puncture speeds ≥ 2.0 mm/s, the temperature variation trend and short-circuit current stabilize and remain consistent, attributed to the influence of lithium-ion transport in the battery electrolyte.
claims.quote: However, for v ≥ 2.0 mm/s, the current value stabilizes after reaching the maximum and tends to be consistent overall. This behavior is attributed to the influence of lithium-ion transport in the battery electrolyte.

claims.claim: Increasing nail radius from 1.2 mm to 3.5 mm causes maximum temperature to rise from 300°C to over 400°C because the larger short-circuit area generates a heightened short-circuit current and increased Joule heating.
claims.quote: It can be observed that as the penetration radius increases from 1.2 mm to 3.5 mm, the maximum temperature rises from 300 ◦C to over 400 ◦C, showcasing a faster temperature escalation trend. This phenomenon can be attributed to the enlarged radius of the nail, which results in a larger short-circuit area and generates a heightened short-circuit current (as per Eq. (10)). Consequently, Joule heating (Eq. (13)) increases and eventually dominates the temperature rise.

claims.claim: After an ISC event, Li+ diffusion at the electrode/separator interfaces may reach the 'ionic transport limit', which diminishes the rate of battery electrochemical reactions and ultimately influences the peak short-circuit current.
claims.quote: Therefore, following an ISC event, Li+ diffusion at the electrode/separator interfaces may reach the 'ionic transport limit', diminishing the rate of battery electrochemical reactions and ultimately influencing the peak short-circuit current.

claims.claim: Heat transfer across the cell's layer structure is almost unaffected by SOC due to low thermal conductivity of the separator and active materials in the thickness direction, but high thermal conductivity of metal current collectors propagates heat along the width direction.
claims.quote: The heat transfer across the cell's layer structure is almost unaffected by SOC, likely due to the low thermal conductivity of the separator and active materials in the thickness direction. However, the high thermal conductivity of metal current collector may propagate the heat transfer along the width direction, thereby increase the TR risk from the short-circuit area to the normal area.

claims.claim: From penetration depth d = 0.2 mm to 0.5 mm, the temperature difference between the nail-penetrated area and Region 1 can reach 250°C, and approximately 300°C with Region 2.
claims.quote: From d = 0.2 mm to 0.5 mm, the temperature difference between the nail-penetrated area and Region 1 can reach 250 ◦C, and approximately 300 ◦C with Region 2.

claims.claim: The maximum electrode current density decreases with each successive unit cell penetrated: approximately 3×10⁹ A/m² in the first unit cell, 2×10⁹ A/m² in the second, and approximately 1×10⁹ A/m² in the third.
claims.quote: the maximum electrode current density in the second unit cell (2e9A/m2) is less than that in the first unit cell, indicating that the newly formed short-circuit currents are smaller than the first unit cell. Similarly, the current density from Al to Cu in the third unit cell peaks at about 1e9A/m2 when d = 0.944 mm (Fig. 6e), also displaying lower values than the preceding current densities.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway (TR) in lithium-ion batteries is defined as a critical occurrence marked by an escalation in temperature triggered by exothermic reactions that may lead to fumes, fire, or explosions.
claims.quote: TR is a critical occurrence marked by an escalation in temperature triggered by exothermic reactions that may lead to fumes, fire, or explosions in lithium-ion batteries.

claims.claim: If sufficient heat cannot be dissipated to the environment during charging or discharging, a temperature rise leads to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: If sufficient heat cannot be dissipated to the environment, a temperature rise is noticed, which leads to a phenomenon known as TR.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the highest TR final temperature simulated was 541.6°C, which was higher than at lower SOC conditions.
claims.quote: a battery pack at 100 % SOC will undergo heavy cathodic decomposition and reach the highest TR final temperature. Fig. 7 concurs with the notion, wherein the highest TR final temperature was 541.6 °C at 100 % SOC.

claims.claim: Higher SOC increases Gibbs energy stored in the battery, resulting in a higher probability of spontaneous reactions that lead to TR.
claims.quote: When SOC is higher, more electrochemical energy is stored within the battery. Consequently, higher SOCs increase the Gibbs energy, resulting in a higher probability of spontaneous reactions that lead to TR.

claims.claim: High charging currents evoke metallic lithium plating on the anode surface, which promotes dendrite formation, pierces the separator, causes an internal short circuit, and generates heat.
claims.quote: High charging currents evoke metallic Li plating. Metallic Li then attaches to the surface of the anode in a process called metallic Li plating. Metallic Li plating promotes dendrite formation and pierces through the separator, causing an ISC that generates heat.

claims.claim: At a charging current of 6.75 A, the highest TR temperature was 940.26°C and the metallic Li deposition rate was 3.68 mol/m³s, compared to 659.91°C and 2.94 mol/m³s at 2.25 A.
claims.quote: the highest TR temperature and metallic Li deposition rate reported was 940.26 °C and 3.68 mol/m3s at 6.75 A. However, Fig. 8a and b also contradict the proposed descriptions given in Eq. (17) because a low charging current of 2.25 A produced the second-highest TR final temperature and metallic Li deposition of 659.91 °C and 2.94 mol/m3s, respectively.

claims.claim: Low charging current contributes to sluggish charge transfer kinetics and low diffusivities of Li-ions, allowing ample time for ions to reduce to metallic lithium and promoting Li deposition.
claims.quote: Lower charging current contributes to the sluggish charge transfer kinetics and low diffusivities of Li-ions. Therefore, allowing ample time for ions to reduce to metallic Li (shown in Eq. (16)), thus promoting Li deposition.

claims.claim: At a charge voltage of 4.5 V and 100% SOC, the highest TR final temperature was 438.54°C, while 2.5 V resulted in a TR final temperature of 400.02°C.
claims.quote: stage III of TR shows that a 4.5 V and 3.5 V produced a high TR final temperature of 438.54 °C, while 2.5 V resulted in 400.02 °C.

claims.claim: TR reactions initiate at 90°C, and all cells in the simulated domain surpassed this temperature when subjected individually to all working conditions (high current, high SOC, high voltage).
claims.quote: Therefore, the TR temperature is affected by all working conditions since TR reactions initiate at 90 °C, and all the cells in the domain surpassed this temperature when subjected individual to all the working conditions.

claims.claim: The Layer recurrent-NN model achieved lower MSE and RMSE values of 0.840196 and 0.916622, respectively, compared to FF-NN (MSE 0.935236, RMSE 0.967076) and Elman-NN (MSE 0.89586, RMSE 0.94650).
claims.quote: the Layer recurrent-NN model provided a lower MSE value of 0.840196 because the predicted temperature values are closer to the simulated temperature. In addition, the MSE is highly sensitive to abnormal points, namely error > 1, resulting in the MSE of the FF-NN and Elman-NN being higher at 0.935236 and 0.89586, respectively.

claims.claim: The Elman-NN model deviates from the simulated temperature at 1500 s by 8.798 K, whereas beyond 1200 s the model deviates from the true value.
claims.quote: beyond 1200 s, the model deviates from the true value. The deviation of the Elman-NN from the simulated temperature at 1500 s is recorded as 8.798 K.

claims.claim: The optimised Layer recurrent-NN configuration consists of 8 hidden neurons, a logistic hidden activation function, and an identity output activation function, achieving R-values of 0.9926, 0.99566, and 0.99381 for training, validation, and testing, respectively.
claims.quote: The most accurate network from observation is network number 1... The ANN model accomplished a good fit with the training dataset because the data are approximately at the 45-degree line, and the R-values for the training, validation, and testing dataset are 0.9926, 0.99566, and 0.99381, respectively.

claims.claim: Network configuration 3, using a logistic hidden activation function and logistic output activation function, is unsuitable for predicting battery temperature because the logistic function assumes linearity during convergence, causing the profile to become flat when temperature depends on random parameters such as SOC, voltage, and current.
claims.quote: The R-value of network number 3 diverges from the value of 1 for the training, validation, and testing datasets because the logistic function assumes linearity during convergence. Additionally, since the temperature is dependent on the randomness of other parameters (i.e., SOC, voltage, and current), the profile of the training, validation, and testing datasets will become flat.

claims.claim: Under working Condition B (100% SOC, 2.25 A, 2.5 V), the maximum temperature in the battery module was 430.04°C and the minimum was 21.50°C, giving a temperature difference of 408.55°C.
claims.quote: Fig. 12 shows the temperature distribution of each cell subjected to working Condition B. Among the battery modules, the maximum temperature was 430.04 °C, and the minimum temperature was 21.50 °C, located on cells #6 and #4, respectively. Therefore, the temperature difference in this battery module is 408.55 °C.

claims.claim: Radial thermal conductivity in the spirally wound battery cell decreases in the direction normal to the sheets (radial orientation), and is computed as the harmonic mean of layer conductivities weighted by thickness.
claims.quote: The thermal conductivity decreases in the direction normal to the sheets, particularly in the radial orientation. The radial thermal conductivity, denoted as kT,r, is computed using Eq. (8).

claims.claim: At 120°C to 170°C, the battery separator begins to show different degrees of shrinkage depending on the material, thickness, and manufacturing process, resulting in physical contact between cathode and anode and eventually leading to an internal short circuit.
claims.quote: At 120 ◦C ~ 170 ◦C, the battery separator begins to show different degrees of shrinkage depending on the material, thickness, and the manufacturing process. This will result in physical contact between the cathode and anode, which eventually leads to an ISC in the battery.

claims.claim: The DSC test of the separator reveals that it begins to shrink at approximately 100°C and completely melts at approximately 167°C.
claims.quote: The results show that the separator used in the battery begins to shrink at about 100 ◦C and completely melts at about 167 ◦C.

claims.claim: A micro internal short circuit (MISC) was observed to occur before battery thermal runaway, evidenced by an accelerated decline in voltage change rate when battery temperature exceeded 99.6°C.
claims.quote: as the battery temperature exceeds 99.6 ◦C, the absolute value of the voltage change rate gradually increases, indicating an accelerated decline in voltage. At this point, we hypothesize that a MISC has occurred within the battery.

claims.claim: At 140.3°C, the battery voltage experiences a swift drop, signifying the occurrence of a massive internal short circuit within the battery.
claims.quote: As the temperature climbs to 140.3 ◦C, the battery voltage experiences a swift drop, signifying the occurrence of a massive ISC within the battery.

claims.claim: A model that does not account for ISC heat generation produces temperature simulation results lower than experimental values and delays the predicted thermal runaway trigger time after ISC onset near 100°C.
claims.quote: Since the model does not take into account the heat generation by ISC, the simulation results of the temperature are lower than the experimental values after the occurrence of the ISC (near 100 ◦C). Also, the TR trigger time was delayed.

claims.claim: The onset temperature T1 for self-heating of the tested LiCoO2/graphite battery was 94°C, the thermal runaway trigger temperature T2 was 182°C, and the maximum temperature T3 during thermal runaway was 491°C.
claims.quote: the onset temperature T1 for self-heating is 94 ◦C. The trigger temperature T2 of TR is 182 ◦C. The highest temperature T3 during TR is 491 ◦C.

claims.claim: The SEI film metastable layer begins to decompose and generate a small amount of heat at 80–120°C, and this decomposition is considered the starting point for battery self-heating.
claims.quote: At 80–120 ◦C, the metastable layer of the SEI film begins to decompose and generates a small amount of heat. The SEI decomposition reaction is generally considered to be the starting point for battery self-heating.

claims.claim: Aged batteries exhibit faster thermal runaway trigger speed and higher self-heating at the same temperature compared to non-aged batteries.
claims.quote: The results demonstrate that the aged battery exhibits a faster TR trigger speed and higher self-heating at the same temperature.

claims.claim: The proposed multimodal diagnostic algorithm provides early warning of thermal runaway more than one week in advance through unsupervised learning with joint inputs of multimodal data.
claims.quote: a multimodal diagnostic algorithm for battery fault diagnosis is developed to provide early warning of thermal runaway to more than one week in advance through unsupervised learning with the joint inputs of multimodal data after graph feature extraction, dimensionality reduction and fault feature fusion.

claims.claim: Battery faults can arise from electrical, thermal, and mechanical abuse, or production defects, leading to decreased battery capacity, limited power output, or even thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Battery faults can arise from electrical, thermal, and mechanical abuse [2,3], or production defects, leading to decreased battery capacity [4–6], limited power output, or even thermal runaway (TR) [7].

claims.claim: Signal decomposition methods are less effective in distinguishing progressive minor faults and require further refinement to optimize decomposition parameters and fault diagnosis thresholds.
claims.quote: signal decomposition methods are less effective in distinguishing progressive minor faults, and require further refinement to optimize decomposition parameters and fault diagnosis thresholds.

claims.claim: A transmission anomaly is identified when all cell voltages converge to a single point causing the standard deviation to drop significantly, with a threshold ratio of standard deviation to average standard deviation set at 0.1 to reduce missed diagnoses.
claims.quote: the ratio of the standard deviation of the voltage at a single sampling point to the average value of the standard deviation is used as the diagnostic criterion for transmission anomalies. The threshold of this ratio is set to 0.1 in this study to reduce the rate of missed diagnoses.

claims.claim: Sensor anomalies are identified when cell voltage suddenly changes by more than 0.3 V and the difference between cell voltage and the average voltage of all cells at that time is more than 0.2 V.
claims.quote: Sensor anomalies are identified when cell voltage suddenly changes by >0.3 V and the difference between cell voltage and the average voltage of all the cells at that time is >0.2 V.

claims.claim: SOC anomalies, identified as a jump greater than 3%, are handled by linear interpolation.
claims.quote: SOC anomalies, identified as jump >3 %, are also handled by linear interpolation.

claims.claim: The multi-stage charging strategy commonly used in EVs causes voltage fluctuations in response to current switching, providing an opportunity for estimating internal resistance.
claims.quote: the multi-stage charging strategy commonly used in EVs cause voltage fluctuations in response to current switching, providing an opportunity for estimating internal resistance.

claims.claim: The estimated internal resistance derived from charging current switching does not precisely correspond to actual ohmic resistance or polarization resistance, but still meets requirements for cloud-based big data applications without strict adherence to physical accuracy.
claims.quote: Although the estimated internal resistance derived from charging current switching does not precisely correspond to the actual ohmic resistance or polarization resistance, it still meets the requirements for cloud-based big data applications without strict adherence to physical accuracy.

claims.claim: Cells prone to triggering thermal runaway often have internal short circuits and manifest externally as slightly lower voltage compared to normal cells.
claims.quote: Considering that the cells prone to triggering TR often have internal short circuits, they manifest externally as slightly lower voltage compared to normal cells.

claims.claim: The TSM scaling factor alpha is set to 0.8 to improve diagnostic effectiveness for severe faults such as internal short circuits by reducing the weight of anomalies in higher voltage cells.
claims.quote: α is the scaling factor set to 0.8 to improve the diagnostic effectiveness for severe faults such as internal short circuits by reducing the weight of anomalies in higher voltage cells.

claims.claim: Converting the RV signal from time domain to a RV signal decoupled from SOC causes the signal to exhibit stationary characteristics, providing a solid data foundation for Fourier transformation.
claims.quote: By converting a voltage signal into a RV signal and decoupling it from SOC, the signal exhibits stationary characteristics, providing a solid data foundation for Fourier transformation.

claims.claim: Time-frequency images exhibit higher robustness to noise compared to time-series signals.
claims.quote: Time-frequency images exhibit higher robustness to noise compared to time-series signals.

claims.claim: The best results for fault feature extraction using STFT were achieved with a window width of 4 and an overlap width of 3.
claims.quote: After comparing the results at different combinations of the window width and the overlap width, we achieve the best results for fault feature extraction with the window width of 4 and the overlap width of 3.

claims.claim: Using ReLU6 as the activation function in the first CNN layer instead of ReLU achieved successful feature extraction from time-frequency spectrograms, whereas standard ReLU could not effectively extract fault features.
claims.quote: due to the unique characteristics of the input time-frequency matrix, the commonly used ReLU function cannot effectively extract fault features. By replacing the activation function in the first layer with the ReLU6 function, successful feature extraction was achieved.

claims.claim: Applying max pooling followed by average pooling to time-frequency spectrograms with uniformly distributed information shows better performance than other pooling configurations.
claims.quote: Since the input features are time-frequency spectrograms with uniformly distributed information, applying max pooling followed by average pooling to the input features shows better performance.

claims.claim: Among six feature dimensionality reduction and extraction methods tested (ICA, PCA, Spectral clustering, K-means, Sparse coder, KPCA), only KPCA succeeded in the required dimensionality reduction task.
claims.quote: The various feature dimensionality reduction and extraction methods we have attempted are shown in Table 2. It is necessary to introduce a new dimensionality reduction method... KPCA: Success [while all others: Fail].

claims.claim: The fault threshold is set at Dfault = 7.5, where exceeding this threshold is considered indicative of a fault, balancing the false positive rate of normal cells and the false negative rate of faulty cells.
claims.quote: setting the fault threshold at Dfault = 7.5. Exceeding this threshold is considered indicative of a fault. Verification has shown that this threshold effectively balances the false positive rate of normal cells and the false negative rate of faulty cells.

claims.claim: For the TR vehicle, the fault score of cell #76 exceeded the threshold for the first time on April 4, which was at least 10 days before the thermal runaway event on April 14.
claims.quote: On April 4, the fault score of cell #76 exceeded the threshold for the first time, marking the algorithm's initial detection of the fault, which persisted until the occurrence of TR.

claims.claim: The proposed algorithm successfully issued thermal runaway warnings 11, 10, and 8 days before the events occurred for three additional TR vehicles equipped with lithium iron phosphate battery modules, with an average warning time of approximately 11 days.
claims.quote: The proposed algorithm successfully issued thermal runaway warnings 11, 10, and 8 days before the events occurred. Overall, the proposed algorithm demonstrates reproducibility in identifying vehicles at risk of thermal runaway, with an average warning time of approximately 11 days.

claims.claim: The multidimensional recursive algorithm reduced the average distance from normal cells to the centroid from 1.0766 to 0.6488, a reduction of 39.7%, after removing abnormal cells.
claims.quote: We found that the average distance from normal cells to the centroid decreased from 1.0766 to 0.6488, a reduction of 39.7 %.

claims.claim: The multidimensional recursive algorithm improved the discriminative ability for defective cells by an average of 7.34% across analyzed segments.
claims.quote: When analyzing other segments, we found that the overall average discriminative ability for defective cells improved by 7.34 %.

claims.claim: The anomaly in cell #50 resulted in abnormal responses in the entire module, as indicated by the CNN-KPCA frequency domain feature extraction showing high fault feature values for cells within the same module as cell #50.
claims.quote: the cells within the same module as cell #50 also exhibit high fault feature values... The anomaly in cell #50 results in abnormal responses in the entire module, indirectly confirming the earlier hypothesis that the anomaly in one cell can lead to abnormal behavior in adjacent cells.

claims.claim: Using a single TSM feature alone makes it challenging to set a unified threshold for faulty cell isolation despite being able to distinguish anomalous cells from normal cells, necessitating the addition of frequency domain features.
claims.quote: It is challenging to use a single feature to set a unified threshold for faulty cell isolation despite being able to distinguish anomalous cells from normal cells. Hence, this study introduces frequency domain features as a complement to time-scale features.

claims.claim: The unsupervised learning approach reduces computational complexity and costs by eliminating the need for large-scale data labeling and model pre-training, and compared to algorithms that require retraining, the workload can be reduced by up to 50%.
claims.quote: The unsupervised learning approach reduces computational complexity and costs, eliminating the need for large-scale data labeling and model pre-training. Additionally, the algorithm's scalability allows for enhanced feature dimensions without retraining. Compared to algorithms that require retraining, the workload can be reduced by up to 50 %.

claims.claim: When ambient temperature exceeds 425 K, thermal runaway can occur in LNCMO/LTO cells even under low discharge rate of 1.5 C or in airflow with high velocity of 5 m/s.
claims.quote: When the temperature of the surroundings is higher than 425 K, thermal runaway can occur, even under low discharge rate like 1.5 C, or in airflow with high velocity like 5 m s−1.

claims.claim: For LNCMO/LTO cells under 5 C discharge with passive airflow cooling, the ambient temperature threshold for thermal runaway decreases as airflow channel thickness decreases: thermal runaway occurs above 395 K for 16 mm channel, above 380 K for 4 mm channel, and above 360 K for 2 mm channel.
claims.quote: Under the same discharging of 5 C and passive airflow cooling, the cells enter thermal runaway when the ambient temperatures are higher than 360 K, 380 K and 395 K if the thickness of the airflow channel is 2, 4 and 16 mm, respectively.

claims.claim: Under adiabatic conditions, cells cycled at 3 C and 5 C reach maximum temperatures of 766 K and 860 K, which are 91 K and 185 K higher than the peak temperature predicted for the cell cycled at 1.5 C.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature reached by the cell cycled under 3 C and 5 C rate is 766 K and 860 K, which are 91 K and 185 K higher than the peak temperature predicted for the cell cycled under 1.5 C.

claims.claim: At 420 K ambient temperature, cells cycled at 5 C rate show thermal runaway 300 and 700 seconds earlier than cells cycled at 3 C and 1.5 C rates, respectively.
claims.quote: at 420 K, cells cycled at 5 C show thermal runaway 300 and 700 s earlier than cells cycled at 3 C and 1.5 C rates.

claims.claim: The predicted maximum cell temperature of 675 K during thermal runaway exceeded the measured peak of 615 K, attributed to ejecta events carrying away heat and thermocouple surface placement, neither of which is accounted for in published thermal runaway models.
claims.quote: the predicted maximum temperature of 675 K reached by the cell is higher than measured peak of 615 K. This discrepancy might have been caused by two reasons. Firstly, this might be due to the ejecta event during the ARC test... The newly developed model at its current status does not consider the ejecta event... To the best of our knowledge, this complexity has not been taken into account in any published thermal runaway models.

claims.claim: For a 18650 lithium-ion battery at 100% SOC, internal pressure follows a power law vs time and reaches the critical safety valve rupture threshold of 1.5 MPa within 94 ms of thermal runaway onset.
claims.quote: For a battery at 100 % SOC, this state reaches the critical pressure threshold of 1.5 MPa within only 94 ms.

claims.claim: After safety valve rupture, the initial pyrolysis gas jetting is completed in less than 2 milliseconds, with peak central outlet velocity of 450 m/s rising to a maximum of approximately 500 m/s.
claims.quote: The initial peak velocity at the central outlet is 450 m/s, rising to the maximum value of about 500 m/s... the initial jetting of the pyrolysis gas is completed in less than 2 milliseconds.

claims.claim: The initial jet fire following safety valve rupture lasts approximately 6 ms and lags behind the pyrolysis gas injection velocity due to accumulation of combustion heat.
claims.quote: The initial jet fire – which lasts around 6 ms after the safety valve rupture – lags behind the pyrolysis gas injection velocity and demonstrates pronounced downstream propagation.

claims.claim: Two vortex currents initially formed on both sides above the safety valve outlet shift downstream over time, causing downstream propagation of the initial jet fire and changing its flame morphology.
claims.quote: Due to high-speed jet effects, two vortex currents are initially formed on both sides above the outlet of the safety valve, shifting downstream over time. This causes downstream propagation of the initial jet fire and changes its flame morphology.

claims.claim: After the gas jet ceases at t = 100 ms, only confined pores above the battery maintain high temperatures, which will likely induce secondary combustion flames within the battery.
claims.quote: After the gas jet has ceased (t = 100 ms), the external flame gradually disappears, and only the confined pores above the battery maintain high temperatures. This elevated temperature will likely induce secondary combustion flames within the battery.

claims.claim: TR induced by slight overcharge cycles has a longer incubation period than TR induced by thermal or mechanical abuse, and once TR occurs it poses greater hazards and spreads at a faster rate.
claims.quote: In contrast with TR induced by thermal/mechanical abuse conditions, TR induced by slight overcharge cycles has a longer incubation period and involves a certain amount of heat accumulation. Once thermal runaway occurs, it poses greater hazards and spreads at a faster rate.

claims.claim: In a 1×5 NCM622 cell cluster confined space, no TR occurred within the cluster when SOC was below 25%.
claims.quote: They found that when the SOC of the cell cluster was below 25 %, no TR occurred within the cluster.

claims.claim: In a 1×5 NCM622 cell cluster, total gas generation decreased from 17.48 mol to 6.17 mol when SOC was reduced from 100% to 50%.
claims.quote: the total gas generation decreased from 17.48 mol to 6.17 mol when the SOC was reduced from 100 % to 50 %.

claims.claim: Using composite phase-change insulating materials completely inhibited thermal diffusion between lithium cells in a 1×4 cluster, achieving zero TRP within the component.
claims.quote: the use of composite phase-change insulating materials completely inhibited thermal diffusion between lithium cells within the cluster, achieving the goal of zero TRP within the component.

claims.claim: In a 5×9 LIB cluster with aerogel insulation, insulating materials slowed heat transfer but were not fully effective in halting TRP, and all cells eventually underwent TR sequentially.
claims.quote: the findings from these numerical simulations indicated that while these materials could slow down the transfer of heat, they were not fully effective in halting the TRP... TRP prevailed involving all the cells in the cluster in a sequential manner.

claims.claim: Better insulating performance causes higher Tmax and earlier TR onset due to heat accumulation within the battery cluster caused by the insulating material.
claims.quote: the better the insulating performance, the higher the Tmax, and the earlier the time of TR occurs, due to the accumulation of heat within the battery caused by the insulating material with good insulating performance.

claims.claim: In a 5×9 cluster with aerogel insulation, 1 mm insulation was insufficient to suppress TRP, 5 mm provided partial suppression, while both 10 mm and 20 mm effectively inhibited TRP.
claims.quote: 1 mm of insulation material was insufficient to suppress TRP, 5 mm provided partial suppression, while both 10 mm and 20 mm effectively inhibited TRP, offering sufficient time for BMS system warnings and post-incident handling.

claims.claim: As ambient temperature increases from -20°C to 40°C in a 5×9 LIB cluster, TR start time decreases from 8660 s to 5990 s and TRP interval time decreases from 7370 s to 4080 s.
claims.quote: as the ambient temperature increases, the TR start time decreases gradually from 8660 s at −20°C to 5990 s at 40°C, while the interval time decreases from 7370 s to 4080 s.

claims.claim: It is recommended to maintain environmental temperature around commercial LIB modules below 20°C during charge and discharge cycles to mitigate risks associated with TRP.
claims.quote: It is recommended to maintain the environmental temperature around commercial LIB modules to below 20°C during charge and discharge cycles to mitigate the risks associated with TRP.

claims.claim: In a 3x3 pack of LFP 18650 cells under adiabatic conditions, releasing 100% of a single cell's electrochemical energy (17.28 kJ) over 10 s does not cause the abused cell to reach decomposition reaction onset temperature, and no TRP occurs.
claims.quote: Fig. 4(b) also shows that the electrochemical energy available in one cell is not enough to increase the abused cells temperature to the point of decomposition reaction onset. Hence, it can be summarized that packs of LFP cells not electrically connected are resilient to TR propagation under the external shorting of a single cell.

claims.claim: Under adiabatic conditions, an instantaneous temperature rise of 250°C in a corner cell of a 3x3 LFP pack causes TRP throughout the pack, with the abused cell reaching approximately 675°C average temperature.
claims.quote: Under abuse by an instantaneous temperature rise of 250°C in Cell 7 (to ensure TR occurs in the abused cell) Fig. 5(a) shows that TR throughout the pack does occur when the pack is under adiabatic conditions... whilst reaching an average temperature of approximately 675°C.

claims.claim: When external boundary heat transfer (convection and radiation to an environment at 60°C) is considered, TRP does not occur even after an instantaneous 250°C temperature rise in the abused cell, with Cells 4 and 8 decomposing only 15% compared to ~40% in the adiabatic case.
claims.quote: due to the heat loss from the pack, the initial decomposition (by 15%) of Cells 4 and 8 is less than the adiabatic case as they reach a lower peak temperature than before. Further the remaining cells do not reach a temperature to lead and decomposition of the cells... hence no TRP.

claims.claim: Excluding tab connections from the 2D model causes all non-initiation cells to decompose simultaneously rather than sequentially, and the resulting timescales for TRP are likely a large overestimate compared to a physical pack with tab connections.
claims.quote: without heat transfer paths through tab connections, this leads to all but the initiation cell to go into TR simultaneously, and not a propagation of cells one after the other going into TR. It should be noted that the time scales leading to TRP are most probably are large over estimate compared to what would occur in a physical pack or if the cell tabs were considered in the model.

claims.claim: Tab connections have been shown to be a significant heat transfer path that can mean the difference between predicting a safe outcome when not considered and predicting an unsafe TRP outcome when considered.
claims.quote: With regards to the inclusion of tabs, it has been shown that they are a significant heat transfer path, and can mean the difference between predicting a safe out come if not considered and predicting an unsafe, TRP, outcome if considered.

claims.claim: SEI layer decomposition on graphite anodes typically occurs at 80–120°C and generates C2H4, CO2, and O2.
claims.quote: The first exothermic reaction is the decomposition of solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer, typically occurring at 80–120°C. When the SEI layer decomposes at 90–120°C, C2H4, CO2 and O2 are generated.

claims.claim: The heat release ranking of cathode material reactions with electrolyte is: LCO > NCA > NCM > LMO > LFP.
claims.quote: The heat release of reaction for different cathode materials with electrolytes ranks as follows: LCO>NCA>NCM>LMO>LFP.

claims.claim: LiFePO4 cathode has an exothermic decomposition onset temperature as high as 310°C, making it a relatively safe cathode material.
claims.quote: LiFePO4 is a relatively safe cathode material, and its exothermic starting point is as high as 310°C.

claims.claim: When the ISC area is large and persists long enough, generated heat may exceed a threshold that triggers abuse reactions producing more heat, causing ISC to propagate and leading to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: if the ISC area is large and persists long enough, the generated heat may exceed a threshold, triggering abuse reactions that produce more heat, causing the ISC to propagate and leading to TR.

claims.claim: In a 100% SOC 18650 cell undergoing thermal runaway, 78.3% of the total energy released (74.9 kJ) was transferred by ejecta and vented gases rather than by conduction through the cell case.
claims.quote: Using LG 18650 MJ1 as an example, 78.3 % of the total energy released in TR (74.9 kJ) was transferred by the ejecta and vented gasses.

claims.claim: ARC experiments showed that the heating rate of NCA cathode cells is four orders of magnitude higher than that of LFP cathode cells.
claims.quote: Their findings revealed a heating rate for NCA that is four orders of magnitude higher than that of LFP.

claims.claim: Calendar aging of lithium-ion batteries delays the onset of cell self-heating but causes thermal runaway to start at lower temperatures due to a thicker SEI layer impeding lithium ion diffusion.
claims.quote: Results indicate that calendar aging delays the onset of cell self-heating, with TR starting at lower temperatures due to a thicker SEI layer impeding lithium ion diffusion.

claims.claim: In a 25-cell module, the cell-to-cell gap, overhead gap, and vent hole location on the cell significantly affected thermal runaway propagation to neighboring cells via hot vent gas flow.
claims.quote: Findings indicated that the cell-to-cell gap, overhead gap, and vent hole location on the cell significantly affected TRP to neighboring cells.

claims.claim: Convective heat transfer from VOC and H2 combustion accounts for 82% of the preheating process leading to thermal runaway propagation in an EV battery module.
claims.quote: Results indicated that convective heat transfer from VOC and H2 combustion, accounting for 82 % of the preheating process to TR, is crucial for TRP.

claims.claim: Without top vent panels in a containerized battery energy storage system, the container faces unacceptable overpressure and external hazards including jet flames and high-speed airflow during a gas explosion event.
claims.quote: Findings indicated that without top vent panels, the container would face unacceptable overpressure and external hazards including jet flames and high-speed airflow.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal runaway (T2) obtained in the oven test is 48.1°C lower than that obtained in the adiabatic thermal runaway (ARC) test for a 24 Ah Li(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)O2 pouch cell.
claims.quote: The onset temperature of thermal runaway (T2) obtained in the oven test is 48.1 ◦C lower than that obtained in the adiabatic thermal runaway test.

claims.claim: T3 (maximum thermal runaway temperature) at the battery surface is approximately half that at internal positions (center, JR_a, JR_b) under adiabatic test conditions, due to heat consumption along the transfer path through jelly roll and shell.
claims.quote: T3 at the surface is approximately half that at the other positions. This is because part of the heat consumption in the heat transfer path occurs on the jelly roll and shell, which causes the temperature to decrease.

claims.claim: Under adiabatic conditions, the maximum temperature rates at internal jelly roll nodes (JR_a and JR_b) are approximately an order of magnitude higher than those at the center and surface positions.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature rates of JR_a and JR_b are approximately an order of magnitude higher than those at the center and surface.

claims.claim: When battery thickness increases to 10 cm under oven test conditions, the difference in T2 values between JR_a and the center exceeds 20% of the center value, caused by a change in TR trigger sequence from JR_b→JR_a to JR_a→JR_b above 5 cm thickness.
claims.quote: The difference in T2 values between JR_a and the center is the largest, exceeding 20% of the center value. In fact, the values at the center and JR_b are not caused by the heat generated inside the battery, but instead result from the large-scale high-temperature heat transfer.

claims.claim: Under oven test conditions, the sequence in which thermal runaway is triggered changes from center-first to surface-first as oven temperature increases from 150°C to 200°C, causing T2 at the surface to increase and T2 at the center to decrease with rising oven temperature.
claims.quote: The sequence by which TR was triggered inside the battery changed from center → surface to surface → center with the increase in oven temperature, leading to an increase in T2 at the surface and a decrease in T2 at the center.

claims.claim: Under adiabatic test conditions, T2 at different positions remains almost constant as battery thickness increases from 1.44 cm to 15 cm, while T3 increases at JR_a, JR_b, and center but decreases by approximately 30°C at the surface.
claims.quote: T2 at different positions remains almost constant with the increase in thickness... T3 at the surface shows a different trend and decreases by approximately 30 ◦C with the increase in battery thickness. This is because most of the heat flow from the inside to the outside is consumed in the transfer path when the thickness of the battery increases.

claims.claim: The SEI film generally begins to decompose at 90–120°C, with metastable components decomposing and transforming into steady-state substances such as Li2CO3.
claims.quote: The SEI film generally begins to decompose at 90–120 ◦C. The metastable components in the SEI film will decompose and transform into steady-state substances such as Li2CO3.

claims.claim: Between 120 and 250°C, SEI film re-decomposition and regeneration reactions co-occur in lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: Between 120 and 250 ◦C, the SEI film re-decomposition and regeneration reactions co-occur.

claims.claim: When the separator melts at approximately 130–165°C, the lithium battery short-circuits and the battery temperature rises rapidly above 300°C.
claims.quote: When the temperature reaches about 130 ~ 165 ◦C [73,74], the separator melts, causing the lithium battery to short-circuit. Various chemical reactions coincide, causing the battery temperature to rise rapidly above 300 ◦C.

claims.claim: The binder decomposition reaction is the primary pathway for H2 production during thermal runaway, and since the lower explosion limit of H2 is only 4.0%, even a small amount can cause fire and explosion.
claims.quote: Although the binder decomposition reaction contributes little to the heat of the thermal runaway process [85], it is the primary way to produce H2 during the thermal runaway process, and the production of a small amount of HF also accompanies it. Since the lower explosion limit of H2 is only 4.0 %, a small amount of H2 can cause fire and explosion accidents.

claims.claim: Among 18,650 batteries with four electrode materials (LFP, NMC, LCO, NCA), NMC and NCA have the strongest gas generation capacity at 99.33 mmol·Ah⁻¹ and 93.73 mmol·Ah⁻¹ respectively, while LCO and LFP are weaker at 53.14 mmol·Ah⁻¹ and 45.45 mmol·Ah⁻¹ respectively.
claims.quote: the gas generation capacity of NMC and NCA lithium-ion batteries is more substantial, and more gas is generated at the same battery capacity, which is 99.33 mmol⋅Ah−1 and 93.73 mmol⋅Ah−1 respectively; the gas generation capacity of LCO and LFP lithium-ion batteries is weaker, which is only 53.14 mmol⋅Ah−1 and 45.45 mmol⋅Ah−1.

claims.claim: For LFP batteries, the proportion of CO2 in generated gas is highest (53%) for 18650 format, while 26650 and prismatic formats produce lower proportions of 25.39% and 25.24% respectively.
claims.quote: Among them, the highest CO2 ratio is 53 % for 18650, and the ratios of 26,650 and prismatic batteries are lower, which are 25.39 % and 25.24 %, respectively.

claims.claim: For LFP 26650 batteries, gas production capacity under overheating conditions (38.65 mmol·Ah⁻¹) is 221.23% higher than under overcharge conditions (12.03 mmol·Ah⁻¹).
claims.quote: The gas production capacity under overheating conditions is 38.65 mmol⋅Ah−1, and under overcharge conditions is 12.03 mmol⋅Ah−1. The gas production capacity under overheating conditions is more substantial, which is 221.23 % higher relative to overcharged conditions.

claims.claim: In NMC batteries, increasing the proportion of Ni increases gas generation capacity and also increases the volume proportions of CH4 and C2H4, with NMC811 showing the highest CH4 (17.39%) and C2H4 (3.98%) proportions.
claims.quote: the increase in the proportion of Ni will also increase the volume proportion of CH4 and C2H4. The volume ratio of CH4 and C2H4 of NMC811 is the highest at 17.39 % and 3.98 %, and the volume ratio of NMC111 is the lowest at 5.88 % and 1.57 %, respectively.

claims.claim: H2 was the first gas detected during thermal runaway in both overcharge and overheating conditions for LFP batteries, with the general gas emission order being H2, VOCs, C2H4, CO, CO2.
claims.quote: H2 was the first gas to be detected during the thermal runaway process... The gas emission order of soft-pack batteries and prismatic batteries was roughly: H2, VOCs, C2H4, CO, CO2.

claims.claim: Higher charging rates cause earlier gas generation in LFP soft-pack batteries during thermal runaway, with gas generation times of approximately 370 s, 460 s, 1150 s, and 1380 s for 2C, 1.5C, 1C, and 0.5C rates respectively, and gas concentration decreasing with lower rates.
claims.quote: high-rate charging rates caused the battery to generate gas earlier. The time for gas generation at the four charging rates was approximately 370 s, 460 s, 1150 s, and 1380 s, and the concentration of the generated gas also gradually decreased.
