claims.claim: Most current gas detection studies detect gas outside the battery shell, meaning the measured initial concentration change represents accumulation to a certain threshold inside the battery, and the initial gas generation inside the battery has not yet been effectively detected.
claims.quote: These methods detect the gas outside the shell, and the initial concentration change of the measured gas is the result of accumulation to a certain concentration inside the battery. The initial gas generation inside the battery has not yet been effectively detected.

claims.claim: A WO3 sensor embedded with 1.5% Pd had the best EMC detection performance at 275°C, with a 45% response to EMC concentrations as low as 100 ppb and a response time of only 19 s.
claims.quote: WO3 embedded with 1.5 % Pd had the best gas detection effect at 275℃and still had a 45 % response to EMC as low as 100 ppb. The response time was only 19 s, which could achieve rapid detection of the initial leakage of electrolyte vapor in the early stage of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The offline detection method can accurately measure the type and concentration of gas but cannot obtain information such as the order of gas generation, gas generation rate, and changes over time.
claims.quote: The offline detection method can accurately measure the type and concentration of gas and analyze the total amount of gas generated during the thermal release process. However, it cannot obtain information such as the order of gas generation, gas generation rate, and changes over time, and it cannot analyze the gas generation process.

claims.claim: For LFP batteries, the LTO battery produced the highest concentration of CO2 and the lowest concentrations of H2, CH4, and C2H2, while the LFP battery produced the highest concentrations of H2, C2H2, C2H4, and C2H6 along with the lowest concentration of CO.
claims.quote: the LFP battery produced the highest concentrations of H2, C2H2, C2H4, and C2H6, along with the lowest concentration of CO; the NMC battery produced the highest concentrations of CO and CH4, but the lowest concentrations of C2H4 and C2H6; and the LTO battery produced the highest concentration of CO2 and the lowest concentrations of H2, CH4, and C2H2.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of LFP batteries under overcharging, the safety valve opens due to release of internal gas, whereas under overheating conditions the safety valve opens due to electrolyte vapor.
claims.quote: They believed that the safety valve pressure relief of LFP during overcharging was caused by the release of internal gas, while under overheating conditions, the safety valve was opened due to electrolyte vapor.

claims.claim: For 18650 NCA cells, the SEI layer may decompose first at about 69°C under thermal abuse conditions.
claims.quote: The solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer, formed on the graphite anode due to the irreversible electrochemical decomposition of electrolyte, may decompose firstly at about 69 °C

claims.claim: The separator shrinks at about 130°C, causing cathode and anode contact and internal short circuit in 18650 NCA cells.
claims.quote: The cathode and anode contact together and the internal short circuit will occur once the separator shrinks at about 130 °C

claims.claim: For LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA) cathode, the onset temperature of exothermic decomposition reactions releasing oxygen is around 160°C.
claims.quote: the onset temperature of which is around 160 °C for LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA) cathode studied in this work

claims.claim: The vent valve of the 18650 cell opens at a critical internal pressure of 1.9 MPa.
claims.quote: When the internal pressure of the cell increases to a critical pressure of Pcir = 1.9 MPa for the currently considered 18650 cell, the vent valve opens to release the generated gases and aerosols of electrolyte vapor.

claims.claim: Neglecting radiative heat loss from solid particles and soot may cause 20–40% over-prediction in jet fire temperatures.
claims.quote: the radiative heat loss from the solid particles and soot was neglected in the present simulation. This omission might incur 20–40% over prediction in the jet fire temperatures.

claims.claim: TR onset temperatures decrease with increasing SOC: 226°C at 25% SOC, 215°C at 50% SOC, and 190°C at 75% SOC for NCA 18650 cells under thermal abuse.
claims.quote: the TR onset temperatures are 226 °C, 215 °C and 190 °C for the cell with 25% SOC, 50% SOC and 75% SOC, respectively

claims.claim: Peak heat release rates of the jet fire increase with SOC: 0.16 kW at 25% SOC, 0.49 kW at 50% SOC, and 1.24 kW at 75% SOC for 18650 NCA cells.
claims.quote: the peak HHRs for 25% SOC, 50% SOC and 75% SOC are 0.16 kW, 0.49 kW and 1.24 kW respectively

claims.claim: During the second venting event (TR), the 25% SOC cell exhibits subsonic flow with peak Mach number of 0.848 and peak velocity of 148.1 m/s, while cells at 50% and 75% SOC exhibit choked flow with peak velocity of 178.3 m/s.
claims.quote: The gas flow for the cell with 25% SOC is subsonic with the peak Ma of 0.848 as shown in Fig. 13 (a), and the peak speed of gas flow at Level 2 is about 148.1 m s−1. The peak Ma of gas flows for the cells with 50% SOC and 75% SOC all reach 1.0, indicating their flows are choked flow, and their peak flow velocity is 178.3 m s−1.

claims.claim: At the safety valve opening of 18650-type Li(Ni0.5Co0.2Mn0.3)O2/graphite cells, the canister pressure increases by approximately 100 kPa and the corresponding gas release is approximately 10 mmol.
claims.quote: The pressure increase is about 100 kPa at the safety valve opening, and the corresponding gas release is approximately 10 mmol.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC 18650-type NCM/graphite cells, the peak canister pressure recorded before O-ring seal collapse during thermal runaway is 2.566 ± 0.381 MPa, while for 0% SOC cells this value decreases to 1.047 ± 0.179 MPa.
claims.quote: The peak pressure of 100% SOC cells is as high as 2.566 MPa while this value decreases to 1.047 MPa for 0% SOC cells.

claims.claim: The gas generation rate during thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries is approximately proportional to the temperature increase rate, across all SOC conditions tested.
claims.quote: The gas generation rate is approximately proportional to the temperature increase rate, which facilitates the simulation on the gas generation because temperature is easier to measure.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC cells in Stage III, the activation energy of the gas generation reaction (Eg = 1.718 ± 0.235 × 10^5 J/mol) is close to the activation energy of the heat generation reaction (Eh = 1.930 ± 0.174 × 10^5 J/mol), indicating an inherent link between these two processes.
claims.quote: for 100% SOC cells, the activation energy of the gas generation reaction (Eg) is 1.718 ± 0.235 × 10^5 J mol−1, which is close to the activation energy of the heat generation reaction (Eh = 1.930 ± 0.174 × 10^5 J mol−1).

claims.claim: The transition temperature between Stage II and Stage III gas generation kinetics is 188.6 ± 4.1 °C for 100% SOC cells and 211.6 ± 2.7 °C for 50% SOC cells, corresponding to the onset of cathode-electrolyte reaction.
claims.quote: The transition temperatures between these two stages for 100% and 50% SOC cells are 188.6 ± 4.1 and 211.6 ± 2.7 °C, respectively. This transition temperature agrees with the temperature of the reaction between cathode and electrolyte.

claims.claim: During Stage IV (thermal runaway), the gas temperature assumption of Eq. (1) is not applicable because gases inside the canister become turbulent and highly heterogeneous in temperature distribution, preventing reliable kinetics parameter calculation.
claims.quote: when TR occurs, the battery bursts and the hot gases eject rapidly. The gases inside the canister become turbulent and highly heterogeneous on temperature distribution. Therefore, the assumption of the gas temperature as derived in Eq. (1) is not applicable in Stage IV.

claims.claim: The safety valve opening temperature for 18650-type NCM/graphite cells decreases from approximately 155.5 °C to 143.9 °C as SOC increases from 0% to 100%, because higher SOC increases the thermal activity of anode-electrolyte and cathode materials.
claims.quote: The opening temperature is approximately equal to 150 °C and increases slightly from 143.9 °C to 155.5 °C as the SOC decreases from 100% to 0%. As SOC rises, the thermal activity of the above materials increases; therefore, the opening temperature of the safety valve shows a decreasing trend with the ascending of SOC.

claims.claim: Electrolyte vapours dominate gas release from lithium-ion batteries before thermal runaway, while generated reaction gases become the major release after the burst of chain reactions.
claims.quote: the electrolyte vapours are found to dominate the gas release before TR, while the generated reaction gases become the major release after the burst of chain reactions.

claims.claim: For a 27 Ah prismatic LiFePO4 battery undergoing thermal runaway, the total simulated mass loss was 139.96 g, with ejected electrolyte vapours, generated gases, and solid particles contributing 70.69 g (50.5%), 15.44 g (11.1%), and 53.83 g (38.4%) respectively.
claims.quote: Eventually, the total mass loss was simulated as 139.96 g, where the ejected electrolyte vapours, generated gases and solid particles contributed 70.69 g, 15.44 g and 53.83 g, respectively, accounting for 50.5%, 11.1% and 38.4% of the whole.

claims.claim: The liquid electrolyte was almost wholly transformed before thermal runaway, meaning no electrolyte vapours are ejected during the thermal runaway stage itself.
claims.quote: it is worth noting that the electrolyte was almost wholly transformed before TR. Therefore, no electrolyte vapours are being ejected for the TR stage.

claims.claim: Ejected high-temperature particles from batteries undergoing thermal runaway can ignite combustible gases released by adjacent batteries, serving as an ignition medium for fire propagation in battery packs.
claims.quote: the ejected particles from the other TR batteries are easily scattered and serve as a medium for igniting combustible gas due to the higher temperature and kinetic energy, which accounts for the phenomenon namely fire propagation.

claims.claim: Particles smaller than 10 μm diameter remained suspended in air for more than 2000 seconds after lithium-ion battery thermal runaway venting, indicating prolonged inhalation hazard.
claims.quote: Even after 2000 s, most of the particles had been deposited on the ground, but there were still a few small particles with a diameter <10 μm suspended in the air, indicating the prolonged hazard of inhalable particulate matters after the LIB TR accident.

claims.claim: Increasing ventilation rate from 100 to 300 L/h increased the fraction of ejected battery particles discharged via exhaust from 17.5% to 32.0% of total particle mass, while reducing peak suspended particle mass from 34.6 g to 14.9 g.
claims.quote: there was also a growing trend for the particles finally discharged from the exhaust fan as the ventilation rates were enhanced, which were 9.0 g, 13.3 g and 17.3 g for 100 L⋅h−1, 200 L⋅h−1 and 300 L⋅h−1, accounting for 17.5%, 24.9% and 32.0% of the total mass... the peak mass of particles suspending in the air was observed to reduce, which were 34.6 g, 21.8 g and 14.9 g for the flow rate of 100 L⋅h−1 and 300 L⋅h−1.

claims.claim: The ejection energy during thermal runaway venting of the tested prismatic LiFePO4 battery was approximately 47.58 kJ, accounting for about 17.69% of the total self-heating energy released during thermal runaway (approximately 268.91 kJ).
claims.quote: The ejection energy is calculated as approximately 47.58 kJ... the amount of self-heating energy from TR can be estimated... calculated as approximately 268.91 kJ. The ejection energy accounts for about 17.69% of the total energy release of TR.

claims.claim: MPI-DeepONet achieves a mean RMSE of 13.2°C for temperature predictions in the test set, significantly outperforming the 25.4°C RMSE of purely data-driven DeepONet.
claims.quote: MPI-DeepONet achieves a mean RMSE of 13.2°C for temperature predictions in the test set, significantly outperforming the 25.4°C RMSE of purely data-driven DeepONet.

claims.claim: MPI-DeepONet requires only 0.5 ms for TR prediction whereas the multiphysics FEM requires approximately 7 s, making it four orders of magnitude faster for LFP 26650 cells.
claims.quote: MPI-DeepONet with encoders requires only 0.5 ms for TR prediction, whereas the multiphysics FEM requires approximately 7 s, making it four orders of magnitude faster.

claims.claim: For NMC 18650 cells, the multiphysics FEM prediction time is 75 s, approximately ten times slower than for LFP 26650, while MPI-DeepONet maintains a consistent 0.5 ms prediction time, yielding five orders of magnitude speed advantage.
claims.quote: the prediction time for MPI-DeepONet with encoders remains at 0.5 ms, consistent with the LFP 26650 discussed in Subsection 4.1, whereas the prediction time for the multiphysics FEM is 75 s, which is approximately ten times slower than that for the LFP 26650. This comparison demonstrates that MPI-DeepONet with encoders operates five orders of magnitude faster compared to the prediction speed of the multiphysics FEM for the NMC cell.

claims.claim: Removing multiphysics constraints from DeepONet causes physically inconsistent asymmetric internal temperature distribution predictions, whereas the multiphysics FEM and MPI-DeepONet produce physically consistent symmetric distributions.
claims.quote: the multiphysics FEM and MPI-DeepONet show a symmetrical internal temperature distribution within the LIB, centered around its midpoint, attributed to the inclusion of isotropic conduction. In contrast, DeepONet produces an asymmetric temperature distribution that is not physically consistent, confirming the effectiveness of supervision from multiphysics constraints.

claims.claim: When the data time interval exceeds approximately 30 minutes (sparse data regime), DeepONet temperature prediction RMSE increases significantly while MPI-DeepONet RMSE remains comparatively stable, indicating MPI-DeepONet's training is not significantly affected by the number of data collocation points.
claims.quote: MPI-DeepONet predicts TR phenomenon more accurately than DeepONet in all cases, revealing that the training of MPI-DeepONet is not significantly affected by the number of data collocation points because MPI-DeepONet is supervised by the multiphysics characteristics of LIBs including thermodynamics and chemical reactions phenomena. Conversely, RMSE from DeepONet increases significantly as the data becomes sparse.

claims.claim: The onset temperature during thermal runaway, defined as the point where self-heating becomes prominent, occurred at t = 184 min and T = 468.9 K, and the crucial temperature, defined as the point where the self-heat rate exceeds 2°C/min, occurred at t = 218.3 min and T = 489.1 K.
claims.quote: the onset temperature, defined as the point where the self-heating becomes prominent, occurred at t = 184 min and T = 468.9 K, and the crucial temperature, defined as the point where the self-heat rate exceeds 2 ◦C/min, occurred at t = 218.3 min and T = 489.1 K.

claims.claim: The MPINN's MAE and RMSE for TR estimation are less than those of an ANN by 0.71 and 1.57, respectively, when fully labeled data are used for training.
claims.quote: the mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean squared error (RMSE) of the MPINN for TR estimation are less than an artificial neural network (ANN) by 0.71 and 1.57, respectively, when using fully labeled data for training.

claims.claim: In the semi-supervised setting, the MPINN outperforms the ANN in terms of MAE and RMSE by 90.56 and 118.64, respectively, when only a small portion of labeled data is used for TR prediction.
claims.quote: It outperforms the ANN in terms of MAE and RMSE by 90.56 and 118.64, when only a small portion of labeled data (semi-supervision) are used for TR prediction.

claims.claim: The MPINN surrogate model evaluates a trained ambient temperature profile in approximately 0.01 s, which is approximately 42,000 times faster than COMSOL Multiphysics, which requires approximately 7 minutes per profile.
claims.quote: After a model is optimized, it can be evaluated on the trained Tamb profiles, which takes approximately 0.01 s per evaluation. This is approximately 42,000 times faster than the computation time of COMSOL Multiphysics, which requires approximately 7 min to compute each Tamb profile.

claims.claim: The positive electrode decomposition model can be predicted by the MPINN without any labeled data with tolerable accuracy, whereas the electrolyte decomposition model requires labeled data for convergence due to a sudden jump in temperature gradient at the thermal runaway point.
claims.quote: unlike the electrolyte decomposition model, which presents large errors around the TR point, the positive electrode decomposition model ensured tolerable accuracy even without supervision. This difference can be attributed to the complexity of landscape loss for different PDEs... a significant change was observed in the gradient at t=224 min in the electrolyte decomposition model... Owing to the sudden jump in the gradient, the complexity of the loss landscape significantly increased, leading to insufficient convergence at a particular point.

claims.claim: Among three feature importance evaluation methods (Random Forest built-in, ANOVA, Spearman correlation), density and viscosity of coolants ranked with relatively high importance, while specific heat capacity ranked highest in Methods 1 and 3 but more average in Method 2, and boiling temperature showed relatively minor importance across all three methods.
claims.quote: Among the three evaluation methods, the impact importance of density and viscosity of the coolants is relatively high, while the importance of specific heat, although the highest in Methods 1 and 3, is more average in Method 2. The boiling temperature of the coolant shows relatively minor importance across all three methods.

claims.claim: Coolants C#1 (Novec 649), C#4 (FC-77), and C#5 (Novec 7500) can effectively mitigate TR induced by tab-overheating, while C#3 (HFE-7200) can only mitigate TR at low C-rates, and C#2 and C#6 completely fail to suppress temperature rise during TR.
claims.quote: C#1, C#4, and C#5 can effectively mitigate the temperature rise of TR induced by the tab-overheating of lithium-ion batteries, while C#3 can only mitigate TR at low C-rates. On the other hand, C#2 and C#6 completely fail to suppress the temperature rise during TR process.

claims.claim: The boiling point of the coolant has a certain impact on the suppression of thermal runaway but is not the determining factor, as the mitigation effect of the 6 coolants on TR does not show regular changes with increasing boiling points.
claims.quote: the mitigation effect of the 6 coolants on TR does not show regular changes with increasing boiling points. While in the similar condition, boiling point of the coolant has a certain impact on the suppression of TR, but it is not the determining factor.

claims.claim: With increasing temperature difference between battery and coolant, the heat flux at the battery surface and tab gradually increases, making coolant temperature difference a key factor affecting boiling heat transfer performance.
claims.quote: With increasing temperature difference, the heat flux at the battery surface and tab gradually increases, indicating that the temperature difference of the coolant is a key factor affecting the boiling heat transfer performance of the battery.

claims.claim: A linear increase in tab current leads to a linear increase in heat generation but results in an exponentially decreasing TR triggering time, indicating that the risk of TR at high charge rates is significantly higher than at low charge rates.
claims.quote: a linear increase in tab current leads to a linear increase in heat generation but results in an exponentially decreasing triggering time (Fig. 7b). This indicates that the risk of TR at high charge rates is significantly higher than at low charge rates.

claims.claim: The piezoresistive sponge sensor achieved a pressure sensitivity of 3.90 kPa⁻¹ with a wide linear range up to 1600 kPa and a coefficient of determination R² = 0.998.
claims.quote: the sensor showed a wide linear range up to 1600 kPa with high linearity (coefficient of determination, R2 = 0.998) and sensitivity of 3.90 kPa⁻¹

claims.claim: Under pre-compression of 0.8 mm, the sensor demonstrated a sensing resolution of 10 μm or less.
claims.quote: The sensor demonstrated a sensing resolution of 10 μm or less, which is appropriate for high resolution strain monitoring.

claims.claim: The sponge sensor compression behavior transitions from a deformation region (compressive strain below 60%) to a densification region (compressive strain above 60%), where adjacent layers expand their contact area causing a sharp pressure increase under minimal deformation.
claims.quote: the compression behavior of the sensor can be divided into two regions: the deformation region (compressive strain below 60 %) and the densification region (compressive strain above 60 %). This phenomenon can be explained by the inter-layer contact within the sponge structure. When the compressive strain of the sponge exceeds 60 %, adjacent layers (i.e., the upper and lower layer) expand their contact area, resulting in a sharp increase in pressure under minimal deformation

claims.claim: Module-level abnormal swelling detection based on the 2nd inflection point of the differentiated swelling signal occurred 1357 seconds before the overcharge-induced thermal runaway explosion in a 1P6S LIB module.
claims.quote: the abnormal swelling detection at t1 provided an early safety warning of 1357 s before the TR-induced explosion at t3

claims.claim: During overcharge of the 1P6S module, the abnormal swelling criterion (2nd inflection point of differentiated swelling signal) was triggered at SOC of only 93.1%, indicating detection at an early stage of the overcharge process.
claims.quote: the developed swelling sensor detected the abnormal criterion at a very early stage, even though the SOC at t1 was only 93.1 %

claims.claim: The swelling sensor signal in the 1P6S module tended to decrease slightly during rest periods due to the negative correlation between temperature and resistivity of CNTs, as battery temperature increases during 1C operation and slowly decreases during rest.
claims.quote: the swelling sensor signal of the 1P6S module tended to decrease slightly during the rest period. This phenomenon can be explained by the negative correlation between the temperature and resistivity of the CNTs

claims.claim: Early TR detection using the swelling signal was much faster than temperature-based detection because overcharge involves a relatively small temperature rise.
claims.quote: early TR detection using the swelling signal was much faster, since overcharge involves a relatively small temperature rise as aforementioned

claims.claim: The Chinese standard GB 38031-2020 requires the battery system to send a thermal event alarm signal five minutes before any passenger compartment hazard is caused by thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: the Chinese standard for electric vehicles traction battery safety requirements GB 38031-2020 requires the battery system to send a thermal event alarm signal five minutes before any passenger compartment hazard is caused by thermal runaway propagation

claims.claim: Cell voltage does not provide a time advantage for thermal runaway detection because it only breaks down during the thermal runaway itself.
claims.quote: The cell voltage provides a clear signal during and after the thermal runaway. Despite being already present in all battery management systems, it does not offer any time advantage as it only breaks down during the thermal runaway

claims.claim: In a parallel connection, cell voltage collapse is delayed until the thermal runaway of the last cell connected in parallel, causing a delayed warning signal.
claims.quote: the voltage in parallel connections collapses after the thermal event of the last cell connected in parallel, meaning a delay of the warning signal

claims.claim: Electrical contact resistances of laser-welded tabs on battery terminals range from 0.13 to 1.25 mΩ, which is of a magnitude that can cause significant errors in temperature estimation for cells with impedances below 1 mΩ.
claims.quote: the electrical contact resistances of the weld seams of the laser-welded tabs on the battery terminals ranging from 0.13 to 1.25 mΩ are of a magnitude that can cause significant errors in the subsequent temperature estimation

claims.claim: For the 2p parallel string of the investigated automotive cell, an impedance measurement accuracy of ±0.0011 mΩ at an impedance of approximately 0.3 mΩ is required to resolve the entire temperature range to an accuracy of 1 K.
claims.quote: for the parallel string in the investigated vehicle, it can be summarized that an impedance measurement accuracy of ±0.0011 mΩ at an impedance of approx. 0.3 mΩ is required to resolve the entire temperature range to an accuracy of 1 K

claims.claim: Temperature estimation errors are less than 1 K for both single cell and 2p module at homogeneous temperature distribution in the range of −10 to 30 °C, but errors increase significantly above 30 °C due to reduced temperature sensitivity.
claims.quote: average error in temperature estimation at homogeneous temperature distribution on single cell and module level of less than 1 K for a temperature range of −10 to 30 °C. Due to the lower temperature sensitivity, the errors become more significant in a temperature range of 30 to 60 °C

claims.claim: In the 2p module with inhomogeneous temperature distribution, the temperature estimation is predominantly driven by the warmer cell, with the estimated temperature falling exclusively within the temperature range of the warmer cell.
claims.quote: it is noticeable that the estimated temperature is exclusively in the temperature range of the warmer cell. The temperature estimation is, therefore, preliminarily driven by the warmer cell

claims.claim: At the single cell level under thermal abuse at 300 W heating, overtemperature was detected 48 minutes before thermal runaway, an impedance anomaly was detected 19 minutes before thermal runaway, and impedance time-derivative exceeded 0.1 mΩ·min⁻¹ five minutes before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: an overtemperature can be detected in Fig. 10(b) just 11 min after the heating process starts, corresponding to a warning signal issued 48 min before the thermal runaway of the cell... The impedance anomaly is detected approximately 19 min before the thermal runaway... the time derivation of the impedance offers a faster detection speed with 5 min before the thermal runaway if the self-heating is sufficient to detect abnormal temperature development with an impedance change > 0.1 mΩ ⋅ min−1

claims.claim: At the 2p module level, the overtemperature criterion could not be detected during thermal abuse due to highly inhomogeneous temperature distribution, but impedance anomaly detection triggered a warning 30 minutes before thermal runaway of the first cell.
claims.quote: the overtemperature at the module level could not be detected due to a highly inhomogeneous temperature distribution. However, an impedance anomaly and collapse on the module level could also be detected, triggering a warning signal up to 30 min before the first cell undergoes TR in the experiment

claims.claim: Above 135 °C, partial separator shutdown cannot be excluded for cells using polyethylene as the mid-layer separator material, contributing to impedance increase during thermal abuse.
claims.quote: a partial separator shutdown can also not be excluded for temperatures above 135 °C (melting temperature of PE, which is assumed to be the mid layer separator material in the investigated cell)

claims.claim: TRP velocity of 7.5 mm/s is correlated with the square root of thermal diffusivity, such that vTR = sqrt(a1*D) where a1 = 5.827 with coefficient of determination 0.999.
claims.quote: The simulation calculations reveal that the TRP follows: vTR = sqrt(a1*D), where a1 = 5.827 and the coefficient of determination is 0.999, demonstrating a highly accurate fit.

claims.claim: TRP occurs when the separator melt failure temperature (Tsep) is reached before the maximum temperature gradient, allowing ISC Joule heating to maintain a high temperature gradient propagating from the hotspot to the normal zone.
claims.quote: The results show that the TRP occurs when the separator melt failure temperature (Tsep) is reached before the maximum temperature gradient, allowing ISC Joule heating to maintain a high temperature gradient propagating from the hotspot to the normal zone.

claims.claim: High-risk TRP fronts are identified by dimensionless risk coefficient f ranging from 1 to 1e5, with cathode reactions and electrolyte decomposition dominating TRP acceleration.
claims.quote: a first-ever dimensionless risk coefficient (f) is introduced to quantify the balance between heat generation and dissipation, identifying high-risk TRP fronts where f ranges from 1 to 1e5, with cathode reactions and electrolyte decomposition dominating TRP acceleration.

claims.claim: A local hotspot alone is insufficient to trigger full-cell thermal runaway; ISC Joule heating from separator melting is required to sustain TR ignition.
claims.quote: A multi-zone ISC model based on energy conservation theory is established, revealing that Joule heating from An-Ca-type ISCs, caused by separator melting, sustains TR ignition, while a local hotspot alone is insufficient to trigger full-cell TR.

claims.claim: Without ISC, the temperature gradient peak at the hotspot boundary does not propagate into the normal zone, whereas with ISC both TRR and TG peaks propagate stably from the TR zone to the normal zone at 1.05 cm in 1.5 s.
claims.quote: Fig. 11(g) and (h) show that with internal short circuits, both TRR and TG peaks propagate stably from the TR zone to the normal zone, moving 1.05 cm in 1.5 s. However, Fig. 11(c) and (d) show that while the TRR peak moves toward the normal zone, the TG peak remains at the hotspot boundary and does not propagate.

claims.claim: ISC heat release is activated only when four conditions are simultaneously met: local temperature exceeds Tsep, remaining electrical energy Eleft > 0, dimensionless negative electrode concentration cNE > 0, and positive electrode conversion degree αPE < 1.
claims.quote: we define the conditions for the ISC heat release to occur as simultaneously satisfying four conditions, namely: (1) The local temperature exceeds the melt failure temperature of the separator, Tsep. (2) The remaining electrical energy Eleft in the battery during the local ISC event is greater than zero. (3) The dimensionless concentration of the negative electrode active material satisfies cNE > 0. (4) The reaction conversion degree of the positive electrode active material satisfies αPE < 1.

claims.claim: Simulations of a 50 Ah NMC532 pouch cell with D = 10 mm2/s and Tsep = 456.15 K yield a TRP velocity of 7.5 mm/s, a TRP front width of 2.8 mm, and a maximum temperature of 690 K.
claims.quote: Simulations demonstrate a TRP velocity of 7.5 mm/s, a width of 2.8 mm, and a maximum temperature of 690 K.

claims.claim: The proposed multiphysics framework achieves computation speeds up to 500 times faster than conventional finite element methods while maintaining total heat generation accuracy within 5%.
claims.quote: resulting in speeds up to 500 times faster than that of conventional finite element methods. Notably, the proposed framework maintains total heat generation accuracy within 5%

claims.claim: In NMC cells, the electrolyte contributes more than 70% of the total heat generation during thermal runaway, while the cathode provides an additional substantial portion, together accounting for over 95% of the total heat release.
claims.quote: In NMC cells, the electrolyte contributes more than 70% of the total heat generation, while the cathode provides an additional substantial portion, together accounting for over 95% of the total heat release

claims.claim: In LFP cells, electrolyte decomposition accounts for more than 80% of the total exothermic heat generation during thermal runaway, because LFP does not participate in significant redox reactions above 300°C.
claims.quote: In the LFP cell, the exothermic reactions differ from those in NMC cells because LFP exhibits higher thermal stability and does not participate in significant redox reactions above 300°C. Consequently, the overall heat release is primarily governed by electrolyte decomposition...which account for more than 80% of the total exothermic heat generation

claims.claim: Internal pressure during thermal runaway is primarily driven by five major gas species—CO2, CO, H2, CH4, and C2H4—which together represent over 95% of the total gas volume, with more than 90% of this gas released during the peak stage of TR.
claims.quote: Internal pressure during TR is primarily driven by five major gas species—CO2, CO, H2, CH4, and C2H4—which together represent over 95% of the total gas volume. More than 90% of this gas is released during the peak stage of TR, accounting for over 95% of the observed peak pressure

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in NMC cells is defined as onset when approximately 8–10% of the total exothermic heat has been generated, corresponding to the concentration of reactive species at the cell core decreasing to approximately 10% of its initial value.
claims.quote: for NMC cells, the onset of TR generally occurs when the cell releases the triggering energy, corresponding to approximately 8% of the total exothermic heat generation. This phenomenon aligns with the stage at which the concentration of reactive species in the cell core has decreased to approximately 10% of its initial value

claims.claim: For LFP cells, thermal runaway onset corresponds to the release of approximately 25–30% of the total exothermic heat, occurring when the concentration of reactive species in the cell core decreases to approximately 30% of its initial value.
claims.quote: for LFP cells, the TR onset corresponds to the release of approximately 25% of the total exothermic heat [28], which occurs when the concentration of reactive species in the cell core decreases to ~30% of its initial value (ccenter = 0.3)

claims.claim: After the onset of thermal runaway, the surface-to-center temperature ratio in cylindrical and prismatic NMC cells remains nearly constant at approximately 1.48 (standard deviation 0.03) across varying convective boundary conditions, because internal heat generation dominates over external heat removal.
claims.quote: In the stage after the onset of TR, the STC ratios remained constant with a mean of 1.48 and a standard deviation of 0.03...At this stage, the rate of internal heat generation significantly exceeded the rate of heat removal, even under cooling conditions. Consequently, the cooling effect had a minimal influence on the internal thermal gradient, and the STC ratio remained constant.

claims.claim: When a 2 mm polypropylene casing surrounds the LFP 4680 cell, the surface-to-center temperature ratio increases to approximately 1.65, compared to approximately 1.45 without casing, because the casing acts as an additional heat sink.
claims.quote: the STC ratio increased to ~1.65 with the casing. This increase is attributed to the casing providing an additional conductive pathway that effectively acts as a heat sink from the cell temperature perspective, thereby modifying the surface–core distribution.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is mitigated when the convective heat transfer coefficient exceeds 25 W/m²K, because forced convection dissipates more heat than internal exothermic reactions, preventing sufficient energy accumulation to trigger TR.
claims.quote: an interesting phenomenon was observed that the TR was mitigated under strong cooling conditions when the convective heat transfer coefficient exceeded 25 W/m2K...forced convection effectively dissipates more heat than internal exothermic reactions, preventing the cell from accumulating sufficient energy to trigger TR.

claims.claim: Reducing four coupled Arrhenius equations to a single equivalent Arrhenius equation decreases NMC cell computation time from 28 hours to 45 minutes (97% reduction) by increasing the adaptive time step size from 1.69e-8 to 5.19e-7.
claims.quote: the single Arrhenius equation effectively captures the exothermic heat generation during the TR peak yet reduces the nonlinearity...the step size required in the stiff region increases significantly, resulting in a substantial decrease in the overall computational time...decreased the overall computation time from 28 h to 45 min, achieving a 97% reduction

claims.claim: The proposed 0D lumped acceleration method reduces NMC cell computation time from 28 hours to 90 seconds (99% reduction) by increasing the adaptive time step size from 1.69e-8 to 5.30e-5.
claims.quote: the proposed acceleration method, which replaces the full 3D heat gradient with an equivalent 0D heat generation term, increases the step size from 1.69e-8 to 5.30e-5. Consequently, the total computational time reduced from 28 h to 90 s, achieving a 99% reduction.

claims.claim: The assumption of a constant surface-to-center ratio of approximately 1.5 is most appropriate under low Biot number conditions (Bi < 0.1), and becomes invalid when a casing causes the Biot number to exceed 0.1.
claims.quote: the Biot number exceeded 0.1 across all boundary conditions, implying non-negligible boundary conditions. These results suggest that the assumption of s ~ 1.5 in the proposed framework is most appropriate under low-Biot conditions.

claims.claim: The surface-to-center temperature ratio decreases with decreasing state of charge in NMC 2170 cells, attributed to reduced internal heat release at lower SOC lowering the relative contribution of the center node.
claims.quote: the STC ratio decreases with decreasing SOC, which can be attributed to the reduced internal heat release at lower SOC, thereby lowering the relative contribution of the center node.

claims.claim: In the NMC expanded prismatic module (9×2 array), thermal runaway propagation follows the lateral direction (Cell 1 to Cell 6) before the side direction (Cell 10 to Cell 14) because the larger contact surface area in the lateral direction results in lower thermal resistance and higher heat flux.
claims.quote: The side direction, characterized by a larger contact surface area and consequently lower thermal resistance, facilitates a distinct sequential propagation at 900 s. In contrast, the lateral direction exhibits the higher thermal resistance due to the smaller surface area, resulting in the lower rate of heat transfer. Consequently, propagation in the lateral direction is delayed to 1300 s

claims.claim: The proposed framework overestimates peak temperature at the pack scale because it neglects the influence of flame generation, mechanical deformation, and local hot spots.
claims.quote: the proposed framework tended to overestimate the peak temperature, arising from neglecting the influence of flame generation, mechanical deformation, and local hot spots

claims.claim: The calculated equivalent lower explosive limit values for NMC and LFP vent gas mixtures are 9.9% and 6.1%, respectively, falling within the reference ranges of 8–11% and 5–7%.
claims.quote: the calculated equivalent LEL values for NMC and LFP cells are 9.9% and 6.1%, respectively, which fall within the reference ranges of 8–11% and 5–7%.

claims.claim: In NCM811/SiC cells, the internal maximum temperature during thermal runaway reached 1192°C, approximately 400°C higher than traditional NCM111-Graphite chemistries, with TR onset temperature Tonset about 25°C lower.
claims.quote: during the TR of large format NCM811-SiC cells, the internal temperature could reach 1192 °C, almost 400 °C larger than the traditional NCM111-Graphite chemistries, with the TR onset temperature Tonset about 25 °C lower, indicating easier trigger of TR and more severe thermal damages.

claims.claim: The thermal interactions between SiC-electrolyte, NCM811-electrolyte, and NCM811-SiC lead to maximum temperature increases of 318°C, 222°C, and 174°C respectively, compared with NCM111/graphite chemistry, with total heat rising by 29%, 20%, and 17%.
claims.quote: the thermal interactions between SiC-electrolyte, NCM811-electrolyte and NCM811-SiC can lead to maximum temperature increase by 318 °C, 222 °C and 174 °C, respectively, with total heat rising by 29%, 20% and 17%, when compared with the conventional Li[Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3]O2/graphite chemistry.

claims.claim: Replacing SiC-electrolyte kinetics with graphite-electrolyte kinetics reduces total heat generation by approximately 29% (318 J), while NCM111-SiC and NCM111-electrolyte substitutions result in heat decreases of 17% and 20% respectively.
claims.quote: The 'Graphite-Ele' kinetic replacement shows the most obvious influence, and reduces the total heat by about 29% (318 J). While 'NCM111-Graphite', 'NCM111-Ele' kinetic substitutions result in heat decrease by 17% and 20%, respectively.

claims.claim: Replacing anode-electrolyte kinetics with graphite-electrolyte kinetics raises Tonset by 11.7% (from 179°C to 200°C) and reduces Tmax by 27.1%, while cathode-anode kinetic replacement reduces (dT/dt)max by 93.6% with no change in Tonset.
claims.quote: An-Ele... Difference: Tonset +11.7%, Tmax −27.1%, (dT/dt)max +22.6%... Ca-An... Difference: Tonset 0, Tmax −14.8%, (dT/dt)max −93.6%

claims.claim: During NCM811/SiC thermal runaway, the internal maximum temperature (1192°C) exceeded the surface maximum temperature (702°C) by 490°C, and internal (dT/dt)max was almost 20 times the surface value.
claims.quote: The max temperature Tmax inside is 490 °C larger than the Tmax outside, while the internal temperature rising rate (dT/dt)max is almost 20 times the value of the (dT/dt)max outside.

claims.claim: The heating power of mixed NCM811 and SiC samples ('Ca + An test') is significantly larger than the simple superposition of individual cathode and anode samples ('Ca + An sum'), indicating violent thermal reactions between charged NCM811 and SiC materials.
claims.quote: the heating power of 'Ca + An' samples (Ca + An test) is significantly larger than the simple superposition of 'Ca' and 'An' sample (Ca + An sum), indicating violent thermal reactions between charged NCM811 and SiC materials.

claims.claim: In NMC-graphite batteries, total TRP time initially decreases during early aging, reaching 78% of the original TRP time at around 80% SOH, then slightly increases to 85% of the original time at 50% SOH.
claims.quote: the total TRP time initially decreases during early aging, reaching 78% of the original TRP time at around 80% state of health (SOH). During late aging, TRP time slightly increases to 85% of the original time at 50% SOH.

claims.claim: Battery aging accelerates TR onset while lowering the heat release, because thicker SEI in aged cells decomposes earlier releasing more heat, while capacity fade reduces total stored energy.
claims.quote: The lower TR onset temperature is because the growth of SEI layer in an aged cell can produce significant heat by its decomposition. On the other hand, the decline in energy content associated with capacity fade in aged cells reduces the total heat released during TR, thereby producing a lower peak temperature compared to a new cell.

claims.claim: Venting accounts for approximately 67% of total heat release during thermal runaway in large-format cells.
claims.quote: Venting becomes especially critical in large cells, as it can account for about 67% of total heat release.

claims.claim: The fastest TRP occurs at around 80% SOH, where TRP time is approximately 15%–29% shorter than at 100% SOH; at 50% SOH, TRP time decreases by 15% on average compared to a new battery.
claims.quote: the fastest TRP occurs at around 80% SOH, where the TRP time is approximately 15%–29% shorter than at 100% SOH. At 50% SOH, the TRP time decreases by 15% compared to that of the new battery, on average.

claims.claim: Slower electrolyte decomposition consistently accelerates TRP compared to faster electrolyte decomposition across all aging scenarios examined.
claims.quote: slower electrolyte decomposition consistently accelerates TRP compared to faster electrolyte decomposition.

claims.claim: The simulation model does not include lithium plating as a dynamic process and is therefore less suited for scenarios where plating is significant.
claims.quote: Since the model does not explicitly include lithium plating as a dynamic process, it is less suited for scenarios where plating is significant.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition begins at 80°C and is observed to dissolve more clearly at 90°C, causing a significant exothermic peak during the self-generated heat stage of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: SEI decomposition begins at 80°C and is observed to dissolve more clearly at 90°C, causing a significant exothermic peak.

claims.claim: During the thermal runaway stage, the temperature exceeds 140°C, which is higher than the melting point of commonly used PE and PP separators, causing the separator to melt.
claims.quote: the temperature will exceed 140°C. The temperature at this time is higher than the melting point of commonly used polyethylene (PE) and polypropylene (PP) separators, causing the separator to melt.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway events, the temperature differential between the interior and exterior of a battery can reach as high as 520°C.
claims.quote: during thermal runaway events, the temperature differential between the interior and exterior of the battery can be extremely large, and can reach as high as 520 oC

claims.claim: There is a lag of 15–40 seconds between the drop in battery voltage and the decrease in temperature during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Typically, there is a lag of 15-40 seconds between the drop in battery voltage and the decrease in temperature

claims.claim: Monitoring CO2 concentration during the initial phase of thermal runaway enabled early detection occurring 625 seconds before temperature warnings.
claims.quote: Setting a suitable threshold for gas concentration enables the early detection of thermal runaway, occurring 625 seconds before temperature warnings.

claims.claim: Batteries at 50% SOC exhibit the least risk of combustion explosion due to exhausted gases during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: this research indicates that batteries at a 50% SOC exhibit the least risk of combustion explosion due to exhausted gases.

claims.claim: Impedance phase shift begins to increase around 118°C while thermal runaway occurs at 197°C, allowing phase shift to serve as an earlier indicator than temperature alone.
claims.quote: the phase begins to increase around 118°C, and thermal runaway occurs at 197°C.

claims.claim: Mechanical abuse accounts for 18% of EV accidents in China.
claims.quote: Mechanical abuse accounts for 18% of EV accidents in China.

claims.claim: The SOC has little effect on the mechanical properties of the cathode, while the anode mechanical properties are affected by SOC.
claims.quote: The SOC has little effect on the mechanical properties of the cathode, while the anode is affected by the SOC. The anode granular layer becomes denser as the SOC increases, which leads to an increase in its stiffness and strength.

claims.claim: In the mechanical-electrical-thermal coupling model, a short-circuit resistor is activated when the distance between positive and negative current collectors falls below threshold r, with r increasing as SOC increases.
claims.quote: When the distance is less than a set threshold r, a short-circuit resistor Rs is used instead of the equivalent circuit to simulate the ISC. The higher the SOC, the sooner the ISC of battery, so r is set to increase as the SOC increases.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of the TR side reaction in the simulation model is assumed to be 80°C, the termination temperature is 300°C, and the exothermic power is set to 30 mW.
claims.quote: assuming that the onset temperature of the TR side reaction is 80 °C, the termination temperature is 300 °C, and the exothermic power is set to 30 mW.

claims.claim: SEI large-scale decomposition is triggered when cell temperature exceeds 130°C, causing rapid heat build-up and increasing the risk of fire or explosion.
claims.quote: The solid electrolyte interface (SEI) large-scale decomposition is triggered when the cell temperature exceeds 130 °C. The side reactions will cause the battery to heat up quickly, leading to a build-up of heat and increasing the risk of fire or explosion.

claims.claim: The TRTPNN model reduces average MAE and MAPE by 17.6% and 41.7%, respectively, compared to a single neural network model across different experimental conditions.
claims.quote: The proposed model reduces an average mean absolute error (MAE) and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) in different experimental conditions compared to the single neural network model by 17.6% and 41.7%, respectively.

claims.claim: The mechanical-electrical-thermal simulation model cannot simulate the force-electrical-thermal response of the battery under axial compression.
claims.quote: due to the limitation of the simulation model, this model cannot simulate the force–electrical–thermal response of the battery under axial compression.

claims.claim: 313.15 K is the onset temperature of the SEI film decomposition reaction, indicating that battery temperature would rise rapidly and is about to trigger thermal runaway.
claims.quote: 313.15 K is the onset temperature of the SEI ﬁlm decomposition reaction; it indicates that battery temperature would rise rapidly, and is about to trigger TR. Therefore, we set β to 313.15 K.

claims.claim: Average battery temperatures rise with significant spatial variations observed along the length of the battery, suggesting that high-dimensional thermal images can capture a more accurate and representative thermal profile across both space and time compared to scalar temperature measurements.
claims.quote: Robinson et al. [17] reported that average battery temperatures rise with signiﬁcant spatial variations observed along the length of the battery, so this suggests that high-dimensional thermal images can capture a much more accurate and representative thermal proﬁle across both space and time.

claims.claim: A longer window with a higher time resolution (5-s) is beneficial to improving forecasting performance of all networks because it involves more information to capture more representative feature patterns.
claims.quote: These results indicate that a longer window along with a higher time resolution (5-s) could be beneﬁcial to improve the performance of all the networks. Such an observation is expectable, as a longer window with a higher time resolution would involve more precious information to capture more representative feature patterns.

claims.claim: Meta-TRFNN outperforms both Meta-No-Img and TRFNN in all forecasting horizons regardless of charging or discharging conditions on simulated samples.
claims.quote: Meta-TRFNN outperforms both Meta-No-Img and TRFNN in all forecasting horizons, regardless of either charging or discharging conditions.

claims.claim: Although TRFNN achieves relatively good average performance, its stability is far worse than Meta-TRFNN, demonstrating that the meta-learning framework stabilizes forecasting performance.
claims.quote: although TRFNN can achieve a relative good average performance, its stability is far worse than Meta-TRFNN. It demonstrates that meta-learning framework would be beneﬁcial to stabilize forecasting performance.

claims.claim: At high SOC state, the total heat released from combustible gas during thermal runaway can reach three times the total internal heat generation.
claims.quote: At a high SOC state, the total heat released from the combustible gas can reach three times the total internal heat generation.

claims.claim: The internal pressure inside the battery shell during thermal runaway can increase up to 1230 kPa.
claims.quote: The pressure inside the battery shell increases accordingly, up to 1230 kPa.

claims.claim: The opening pressure threshold of the safety valve is approximately 1.5 MPa, above which gas is ejected.
claims.quote: The opening pressure threshold of the safety valve Pvalve is about 1.5 MPa. When the safety valve opens, the gas inside the battery will be ejected under the large pressure difference.

claims.claim: In 18650 NCM batteries at 100% SOC, 6 out of 15 samples experienced intense jet combustion lasting at least 20 s, while the remaining 9 did not form a continuous flame.
claims.quote: Six out of fifteen samples experienced intense jet combustion, which lasted for at least 20 s... The remaining nine samples did not form a continuous flame during thermal runaway, while spark jet and intermittent flame were observed.

claims.claim: The maximum combustion temperature of 1075.4°C was measured at 80 mm above the safety valve, while peak temperatures at 320 mm and 400 mm above the cell were lower than 300°C.
claims.quote: The temperature at 80 mm above the cell is the highest. At 80 mm, the maximum temperature in Cell No. 1 reached 1075.4°C. The flame temperature drops down as the height increases. The peak temperatures at the locations 320 mm and 400 mm above the cell No. 1 were lower than 300°C.

claims.claim: The coupled model overestimated temperature in the latter half of combustion because the simulation only included gas-phase combustibles, whereas in reality combustibles also include electrolyte vapor and combustible solids with slower burn rates.
claims.quote: the combustion material in the simulation model only contained the gas generated from the chemical reaction. In reality, the combustibles also include electrolyte vapor and combustible solids. The burning rate of these materials is relatively slow and last for a longer time.

claims.claim: When heat generation rate in a LIB cell exceeds the dissipated heat to the surroundings, exothermic reactions cause heat accumulation and acceleration of chemical reactions, which can then lead to a thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Several exothermic reactions can occur as the inner cell temperature increases, and if the heat generation is larger than the dissipated heat to the surroundings, this leads to heat accumulation in the cell and acceleration of the chemical reactions, which can then lead to a thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The SEI layer is meta-stable and can decompose exothermically at 90–120°C.
claims.quote: Solid electrolyte interface (SEI) decomposition reaction: SEI layer is meta-stable and can decompose exothermically at 90-120°C

claims.claim: The negative-solvent (NS) reaction between lithium and electrolyte occurs at elevated temperature above 120°C.
claims.quote: Negative-solvent reaction (NS): the reaction between lithium and electrolyte at elevated temperature (>120°C)

claims.claim: The positive-solvent (PS) reaction involves chemical reduction of positive active materials with the electrolyte at further elevated temperature above 170°C.
claims.quote: Positive-solvent reaction (PS): chemical reduction of positive active materials with the electrolyte at further elevated temperature (>170°C)

claims.claim: In large-format LIB cells, the uniformity of the electrical potential along the current collectors in the cell composites is no longer given, and thermal dynamics must also be taken into account due to the inhomogeneity of the temperature field with respect to the cell geometry.
claims.quote: In large formatted battery cells, the uniformity of the electrical potential along the current collectors in the cell composites is no longer given. Additionally, due to the inhomogeneity of the distribution of the temperature field with respect to the cell geometry, thermal dynamics must also be taken into account.

claims.claim: For NCM523 batteries under high-temperature shock, the onset of self-heating (T1) is defined as when the temperature rise rate exceeds 8 °C/s, and ignition onset (T2) is defined as when the temperature rise rate exceeds 30 °C/s.
claims.quote: T1: The onset temperature of self-heating, Threshold: Temperature rise rate exceeds 8 °C s−1; T2: The onset temperature of ignition, Threshold: Temperature rise rate exceeds 30 °C s−1

claims.claim: Peak heat release rate (HRR) during thermal runaway under high-temperature shock decreases from 165.3 kW at 100% SOC to 7.0 kW at 0% SOC for NCM523 batteries.
claims.quote: the peak HRR under 100%, 70%, 50%, 25%, 0% SOC are 165.3, 108.9, 69.1, 32.2, and 7.0 kW, respectively

claims.claim: For LFP batteries under high-temperature shock, ignition time (t1) and heat source distance in the range 15–60 cm have a strong linear relationship described by y = 0.37x + 5.3, where x is distance in cm and y is ignition time in seconds.
claims.quote: In the range of 15–60 cm, the distance and t1 have a strong linear relationship, using the primary function y = 0.37x + 5.3 can be well fitted, where x denotes distance and y denotes t1

claims.claim: Under open-environment high-temperature shock, LFP battery post-combustion mass loss rate was only 0.31% and maximum back-surface temperature was 43 °C, indicating incomplete thermal runaway, whereas NCM523 battery mass loss rate was 34.3%.
claims.quote: The post-combustion mass is 468.89 g, with a mass loss rate of only 0.31%... the mass loss of LFP battery after combustion is only 0.7 g, with a mass loss rate of 0.15%, which is greatly less than that of NCM523 battery (34.3%)

claims.claim: For NCM523 cathodes, reactions with electrolyte generally initiate upon exceeding 180 °C, whereas for LFP cathodes the onset occurs above 240 °C due to superior thermal stability.
claims.quote: For NCM523 cathodes, these reactions generally initiate upon exceeding 180 °C. For LFP cathodes, the onset occurs at a substantially higher temperature due to their superior thermal stability, typically above 240 °C.

claims.claim: Peak HRR for LFP batteries under high-temperature shock decreases from 169.8 kW at 15 cm to 84.8 kW at 60 cm distance from the heat source.
claims.quote: The peak HRR at 15, 30, 45, 60 cm are 169.8, 133.4, 108.6, and 84.8 kW, respectively.

claims.claim: The validated 3D conjugate heat transfer and TR coupling model predicts T2 for NCM523 with a 16.5% error but predicts maximum surface temperature T3 with only 0.68% error, indicating difficulty capturing the exact ignition instant but accurate energy balance.
claims.quote: T2: Experimental 630.1 °C, Model 526.4 °C, error 16.5%; T3: Experimental 973.6 °C, Model 980.1 °C, error 0.68%

claims.claim: SEI layer decomposition begins at internal temperatures between 80–120 °C, releasing heat and gases including CO2 and C2H4.
claims.quote: begins to decompose at internal temperatures between 80-−120 °C. This decomposition under thermal abuse conditions releases heat and gases like CO2 and C2H4

claims.claim: At 120 °C, the anode directly exposes to the electrolyte due to almost complete decomposition of the SEI layer, leading to exothermic reactions that generate flammable alkanes, with reaction intensity correlating positively with battery SOC.
claims.quote: As the temperature reaches 120 °C, the anode directly exposes to the electrolyte due to the almost complete decomposition of the SEI layer, leading to exothermic reactions that generate flammable alkanes. The intensity of these reactions correlates positively with the battery's state of charge (SOC).

claims.claim: The separator, typically made from polyethylene or polypropylene, reaches its melting point at approximately 130–170 °C, sharply increasing internal resistance and potentially triggering severe internal short circuits.
claims.quote: the separator, typically made from materials like polyethylene or polypropylene, reaches its melting point ≈130–170 °C. The melting of the separator significantly impedes the migration of lithium ions between the anode and cathode, sharply increasing the battery's internal resistance.

claims.claim: Thermal stability of cathode materials follows the order LiFePO4 > LiMn2O4 > LiNixCoyMnzO2 (x ≤ 0.8) > LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 > LiCoO2.
claims.quote: Typically, LiFePO4 (LFP) > LiMn2O4 (LMO) > LiNixCoyMnzO2(NCM, x ≤ 0.8) > LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA) > LiCoO2 (LCO).

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a 25 Ah large-format prismatic NCM LIB, the internal temperature difference remains below 1 °C under normal operating conditions but can escalate to approximately 520 °C during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Feng et al. examined the thermal runaway characteristics of a 25 Ah large-format prismatic LIB with NCM cathode, noting that the internal temperature difference usually remains below 1 °C under normal operating conditions, but can escalate to ≈520 °C during thermal runaway.

claims.claim: CO, CO2, and H2 combined typically comprise over 70% of the total gases produced during thermal runaway of 18650 cells under overheating conditions.
claims.quote: Notably, CO, CO2, and H2 combined typically comprise over 70% of the total gases.

claims.claim: Non-flammable CO2 can represent up to 70% of total gases from decomposition of 1.2 m LiPF6 in DMC/EC/EMC electrolyte mixture, whereas flammable gases like H2, C2H6, and C3H8 dominate decomposition of 1.2 m LiPF6 in DEC solution.
claims.quote: non-flammable CO2 can represent up to 70% of the total gases from the decomposition of an electrolyte mixture of 1.2 m LiPF6 in DMC/EC/EMC, whereas flammable gases like H2, C2H6, and C3H8 dominate the decomposition products in a 1.2 m LiPF6 in DEC solution.

claims.claim: Voltage and impedance signals change more slowly than the rate at which thermal runaway evolves and propagates within the battery, resulting in poor temporal resolution for characterizing battery failure.
claims.quote: These signals often change more slowly than the rate at which thermal runaway evolves and propagates within the battery, resulting in poor temporal resolution for characterizing battery failure.

claims.claim: Mechanical abuse such as nail penetration typically causes an abrupt voltage drop to 0 V, while voltage declines due to electrical or thermal abuse tend to be more gradual and complex.
claims.quote: mechanical abuse, like nail penetration, typically causes an abrupt voltage drop to 0 V, while voltage declines due to electrical or thermal abuse tend to be more gradual and complex.

claims.claim: Placing four acoustic sensors at the corners of an energy storage station, combined with sound source localization methods, can locate a faulty cell with a maximum spatial error of 0.1 m and response times at the millisecond level.
claims.quote: Lyu et al. demonstrated that placing just four acoustic sensors at the corners of an energy storage station, combined with sound source localization methods, can accurately and promptly locate a faulty cell, achieving a maximum spatial error of just 0.1 m and response times at the millisecond level.

claims.claim: Binders such as poly(vinylidenefluoride) and carboxymethylcellulose react with intercalated lithium in cathode/anode materials at temperatures exceeding 260 °C, producing H2 gas.
claims.quote: Binders such as poly(vinylidenefluoride) and carboxymethylcellulose react with intercalated lithium in cathode/anode materials at temperatures exceeding 260 °C, producing H2 gas

claims.claim: Upon persistent internal temperature elevation to approximately 300 °C, continuous reactions generate large amounts of flammable gases causing battery deformation and swelling, with complete thermal runaway potentially reaching internal temperatures of 500–1000 °C.
claims.quote: Upon persistent internal temperature elevation to ≈300 °C, continuous reactions of electrolyte and electrode materials generate large amounts of flammable gases... complete thermal runaway is reached, with internal temperatures potentially reaching 500–1000 °C.

claims.claim: As battery state of health deteriorates—marked by increased internal resistance and degradation of active materials—the onset temperature for cell self-heating decreases, requiring dynamic adjustments to alarm thresholds.
claims.quote: as the battery's state of health (SOH) deteriorates—marked by increased internal resistance and degradation of active materials—the onset temperature for cell self-heating decreases. This degradation requires dynamic adjustments to the alarm thresholds.

claims.claim: Gas signals offer clear advantages over voltage, temperature, pressure, and creep distance signals in terms of response speed, signal strength, and overall reliability for detecting thermal runaway in automotive LIBs.
claims.quote: The findings, detailed in Figure 4b, demonstrate that gas signals offer clear advantages in terms of response speed, signal strength, and overall reliability.

claims.claim: The commercial liquid electrolyte begins to decompose at approximately 150 °C, with decomposition products like PF5 accelerating electrolyte breakdown and reacting with H2O to release toxic HF.
claims.quote: The commercial liquid electrolyte, relatively unstable under high temperatures, begins to decompose ≈150 °C. Decomposition products like PF5 accelerate the breakdown of the electrolyte and react with H2O to release toxic HF

claims.claim: For low-temperature cycling aged cells, the reaction between plated lithium and electrolyte occurs at around 140°C, which promotes reactions and leads to an earlier start of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: for low temperature cycling cells, the reaction between plated lithium and electrolyte occurred at around 140°C, which promoted the reactions and led to an earlier start of TR.

claims.claim: The positive-electrolyte reaction is the largest heat source during thermal runaway, and the negative-electrolyte reaction is the second largest, with the two together accounting for more than 90% of total heat generation.
claims.quote: the positive-electrolyte reaction releases the largest amount of energy and the negative-electrolyte reaction is the second largest heat source. Heat releases from the two sources account for larger than 90 % of the total heat generation.

claims.claim: Total heat generation during thermal runaway increases only a little with ageing state for low-temperature cycling cells.
claims.quote: The total heat generation increases only a little with the ageing state.

claims.claim: After cycling at -10°C for 10 cycles and 30 cycles, the state of health (SOH) of the NCA/Graphite 18650 cells was approximately 83% and 47%, respectively.
claims.quote: After cycling at −10°C for 10 cycles and 30 cycles, the state of health (SOH) was about 83 % and 47 %, respectively.

claims.claim: Thickening of the SEI film improves battery thermal stability, while lithium plating greatly deteriorates battery thermal runaway performance, representing opposite effects on safety.
claims.quote: SEI growth and lithium (Li) plating have important and opposite effects on battery safety... Thickening of SEI film improves the thermal stability of battery... However, lithium plating/deposition greatly deteriorates the battery TR performance.

claims.claim: When a separator fractures under mechanical abuse, anode and cathode materials come into contact, resulting in an internal short circuit that increases the risk of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: When the single battery cell is subjected to mechanical abuse, once the separator is fractured, the anode and cathode materials will come into contact with each other, resulting in an internal short circuit. Therefore, the mechanical integrity of the separator largely determines the risk level of thermal runaway of cells.

claims.claim: Contact between the anode material coating and the cathode current collector (Al) is the most dangerous of the four internal short-circuit modes in terms of electrocaloric response.
claims.quote: It has been documented that these four internal short-circuit modes differ in their effects on the electrocaloric response of the cell, and the contact between the anode material coating and the cathode current collector (Al) is the most dangerous.

claims.claim: In packed cylindrical battery modules, when crushing speed reaches a certain value, batteries deform row by row from top to bottom, meaning the side close to the impact has a higher risk of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: they found that each battery cell deforms uniformly during quasi-static compression. However, when the crushing speed reaches a certain value, the packed batteries deform row by row from top to bottom, which means that the side close to the impact has a higher risk of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: For battery modules compressed in the thickness direction, fracture is less likely than in length/width directions, leading to heat accumulation and greater risk of thermal runaway compared to impacts from other directions.
claims.quote: the structure of the module is more stable when it is compressed in the thickness direction than in the length and width directions. However, the fracture of the material in the length and width directions is conducive to the timely release of heat, while the thickness direction is more likely to lead to heat accumulation, thus inducing thermal runaway, so the impact from the thickness direction is more dangerous.

claims.claim: In cylindrical battery modules, thermal runaway propagation is mainly transmitted through electrical connections, while in pouch battery modules it is dominated by heat transfer between batteries, due to the larger contact area between pouch cells.
claims.quote: the battery module composed of cylindrical cell and the battery pack composed of the pouch cell differ in the main way of thermal runaway propagation. The contact area between the pouch cells is much larger than that of the cylindrical cell, so the heat in the cylindrical batteries module is mainly transmitted through electrical connections, while the pouch battery module is dominated by heat transfer between the batteries.

claims.claim: Silicon-containing composite anode materials can reach up to 400% volume change during lithiation/delithiation, dramatically increasing intercalation-induced stress compared to traditional graphite anodes.
claims.quote: the volume change of these active materials during lithiation/delithiation is particularly pronounced when Si can reach 400%, and the resulting intercalation-induced stress also increases dramatically.

claims.claim: A multi-point short circuit with annular distribution accumulates heat more easily and raises battery temperature more rapidly than a single-point short circuit.
claims.quote: The multi-point short circuit with annular distribution is easier to accumulate heat and rapidly increase the battery temperature.

claims.claim: Lithium plating on the anode caused by low-temperature aging is identified as the most crucial factor in the safety degradation of Li-ion batteries during their whole life cycle.
claims.quote: They pointed out that lithium plating on the anode caused by low-temperature aging is the most crucial factor in the safety degradation of Li-ion batteries during their whole life cycle.

claims.claim: After low-temperature cycle aging, the self-generated heat rate of the battery increased significantly in the temperature range of 30–100°C.
claims.quote: Friesen's studies showed that the self-generated heat rate of the battery after cycle aging at 0°C increased significantly in the temperature range of 30–100°C.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of self-heating (T1) did not change significantly across batteries with SOH ranging from 80% to 95%, indicating that lithium deposited on the anode contributes little to heat generation at the start of self-heating.
claims.quote: the T1 of batteries with different SOHs did not change significantly, which indicates that when the battery begins to self-heating, the reaction between the lithium deposited on the anode and the electrolyte has little contribution to the heat generation of the battery.

claims.claim: Both the pre-exponential factor (lnA) and activation energy (Ea) of TR side reactions increased with decreasing SOH, indicating reduced thermal stability as lithium plating severity increases.
claims.quote: Both A and Ea increased with the decrease of SOH, which indicated that the thermal stability of the battery gradually decreased with the decline of battery capacity and the more severe lithium plating on the anode.

claims.claim: The heat generation process of TR stage 3 (from T2 to T3) is not sensitive to SOH because the lithium deposited on the negative electrode has been fully reacted in stages 1 and 2.
claims.quote: it can be found that the heat generation process of stage three is not sensitive to SOH, and the distribution of results of different SOHs is very concentrated. This is because the lithium deposited on the negative electrode has been fully reacted in stage 1 and stage 2, and the battery capacity decay has little effect on stage three.

claims.claim: For an LCO/graphite pouch cell aged at −10°C to 80% SOH, the TR trigger time (Δt1) was 13,313 s compared to 34,295 s for 95% SOH, showing that lower SOH significantly reduces time to thermal runaway.

After sufficient rest or cycling at room temperature, lithium deposited on the anode may partially react with the electrolyte or re-intercalate, causing partial recovery of battery safety performance.
claims.quote: when after a long enough rest or cycle at room temperature, the lithium deposited on the anode may partially react with the electrolyte or embed back, causing the recovery of the battery safety performance.

claims.claim: At a charge rate of 3C, the maximum temperature during thermal runaway due to electrical abuse was 209.4°C, and the thermal runaway duration was 1200 s.
claims.quote: At charge rate of 3C, the maximum temperature was 209.4 ◦C, since a high current generates large heat, and the thermal runaway duration was 1200 s.

claims.claim: When SOC increased from 0.5 to 1 under mechanical abuse (penetration), the maximum temperature during thermal runaway increased from 192°C to 665°C.
claims.quote: When the SOC increased from 0.5 to 1, the maximum temperature increased from 192 to 665 ◦C.

claims.claim: The SEI layer starts to decompose when battery temperature reaches 100°C during thermal runaway due to electrical abuse.
claims.quote: When the battery temperature reaches 100 ◦C, the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) starts to decompose.

claims.claim: Reactions between electrodes and electrolyte start when battery temperature exceeds 200°C during electrical abuse thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The reactions between the electrodes and the electrolyte start when the temperature is above 200 ◦C.

claims.claim: The separator is damaged above 80°C, followed by internal short circuit; at 3C the battery temperature reaches 80°C fast causing thermal runaway, while at 0.5C internal short circuit does not occur and temperature hardly increases.
claims.quote: the separator is damaged above 80 ◦C, followed by internal short circuit. This means that the battery temperature starts to rise drastically when the temperature over 80 ◦C fast at 3C, and thermal runaway occurs fast as well. While, internal short circuit does not occur at 0.5C, so that temperature hardly increases.

claims.claim: In mechanical abuse thermal runaway, SOC affects resistance more than temperature because at high SOC the potential difference between anode and cathode electrolytes increases, resulting in more generated heat.
claims.quote: the SOC affected the resistance more than the temperature since, at a high SOC, the potential difference between the anode and cathode electrolytes increased, resulting in more generated heat.

claims.claim: The integrated ECM model applicability is limited to SOC < 1 and C-rate < 1 based on available experimental data.
claims.quote: Since the thermal runaway of the battery in this model described based on previous experimental data, the applicable boundary is limited: SOC < 1 and C-rate < 1.

claims.claim: Heat generation during mechanical abuse thermal runaway was approximately 8 kW at SOC of 0.5 and approximately 84 kW at SOC of 1.
claims.quote: which was about 8 kW at a SOC of 0.5 and 84 kW when SOC reached 1.

claims.claim: In overcharging lithium iron phosphate batteries, the voltage range of 4.2 V to 5.2 V is defined as the very early stage of thermal runaway, characterized by a slow increase in surface temperature.
claims.quote: The voltage between 4.2 V and 5.2 V is defined as the very early stage of TR due to overcharging in LIBs.

claims.claim: When the battery voltage exceeds 5.2 V or surface temperature exceeds 50°C during overcharging, the battery is defined as being in the early-mid stage of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: When the voltage exceeds 5.2 V or the temperature exceeds 50°C, it is defined as the early-mid stage of TR.

claims.claim: At higher charging rates during overcharging, the slow temperature rise stage is shorter and thermal runaway occurs faster inside the battery.
claims.quote: the shorter the slow temperature rise stage, the faster TR occurs inside the battery, especially at higher charging rates.

claims.claim: Gas generation during overcharging of commercial lithium iron phosphate batteries was detected when battery surface temperature reached 53°C and SOC reached 120%.
claims.quote: Fernandes et al. conducted overcharging experiments on commercial lithium iron phosphate batteries and found that gas generation was detected when the battery surface temperature reached 53°C and the battery State of Charge (SOC) reached 120%.

claims.claim: Voltage-based thermal runaway detection is limited because a large number of parallel cells suppresses the fault voltage signal, and by the time voltage undergoes sudden change, thermal runaway has already occurred for some time.
claims.quote: a large number of parallel cells in the battery box will suppress the fault voltage signal, and when the voltage signal undergoes a sudden change, thermal runaway inside the battery has already occurred for some time, making it difficult to achieve very early warning based solely on a single voltage change.

claims.claim: Early-stage battery fault diagnosis using gas sensors is feasible but highly dependent on fault condition and battery material.
claims.quote: Essl et al. experimentally verified that early-stage battery fault diagnosis using gas sensors is feasible, but it is highly dependent on the fault condition and battery material.
